standardLabel: Decode multisyllable words using word parts
title: Grade 3 Multisyllable Word Parts Lesson
lessonExplanation: When a word has more than one syllable, break it into smaller parts. Read each part, then blend the parts back together.
workedExample: The word helpful can be read as help + ful. The ending -ful means full of, so helpful means full of help or useful.

choices: care/ful/ly
ca/ref/ully
caref/ul/ly
carefu/lly
guidedPractice.passage: The class found a smooth stone beside the stream. Later, they carefully wrote notes about the shape, color, and size of each object they collected.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which division helps read the word carefully?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: Care/ful/ly shows meaningful word parts that help with reading and meaning.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: care/ful/ly

standardLabel: Read grade-level words with common vowel patterns
title: Grade 3 Short and Long Vowel Patterns Lesson
lessonExplanation: Strong Grade 3 readers use vowel patterns to read unfamiliar words. Look for spelling clues like silent e, vowel teams, and closed syllables before choosing how a word should sound.
workedExample: In the word made, the silent e helps the a say its long sound. In the word match, the vowel sound is short because the vowel is closed in by consonants.

choices: stone
not
drum
went
guidedPractice.passage: The class found a smooth stone beside the stream. Later, they carefully wrote notes about the shape, color, and size of each object they collected.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which word has a long vowel sound?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: Stone has a silent e, so the o makes a long vowel sound.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: stone

standardLabel: Determine main idea and recount key details
title: Grade 3 Main Idea Lesson
lessonExplanation: Main idea is what the paragraph, section, or whole passage is mostly about. Grade 3 target: Identify the main idea in a short paragraph or brief passage. Text complexity: One short paragraph with clear repeated details. Evidence demand: Use one or two stated key details. Reasoning: Tell what the text is mostly about without adding opinions.
workedExample: If most details tell how mulch helped the garden hold water, the main idea is that students found a way to keep the garden damp. A small detail, like carrying buckets, supports the idea but is not the whole main idea.

choices: Students found a way to help the garden hold water.
Students played in the garden.
The garden had many colors.
The class stopped caring for plants.
guidedPractice.passage: The class garden needed more water. Students carried small buckets each morning. Soon they noticed that the soil stayed dry by lunch. They added mulch around the plants, and the soil stayed damp longer. The plants grew stronger during the next week.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Identify the main idea in a short paragraph or brief passage.
guidedPractice.coachHint: Use one or two stated key details.
guidedPractice.explanation: The best answer is "Students found a way to help the garden hold water." because it meets this Grade 3 expectation: Choose the main idea and match it to a directly stated detail.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Students found a way to help the garden hold water.

standardLabel: Make inferences using text evidence
title: Grade 3 Inference Lesson
lessonExplanation: Inference means using text clues plus what makes sense to figure out an idea the author does not state directly. Grade 3 target: Use clear clues to figure out an unstated idea. Text complexity: Short passage with obvious clues. Evidence demand: Use one stated clue. Reasoning: Connect clue plus common sense.
workedExample: If students notice dry soil and add mulch, you can infer they are trying to solve the garden problem.

choices: The students noticed a problem and tried to fix it.
The students wanted the garden to dry out.
The students never checked the plants.
The mulch made the soil disappear.
guidedPractice.passage: The class garden needed more water. Students carried small buckets each morning. Soon they noticed that the soil stayed dry by lunch. They added mulch around the plants, and the soil stayed damp longer. The plants grew stronger during the next week.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Use clear clues to figure out an unstated idea.
guidedPractice.coachHint: Use one stated clue.
guidedPractice.explanation: The best answer is "The students noticed a problem and tried to fix it." because it meets this Grade 3 expectation: Choose an inference that is directly supported by a clue.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: The students noticed a problem and tried to fix it.

choices: The students noticed a problem and tried to fix it.
The students wanted the garden to dry out.

standardLabel: Ask and answer questions using text evidence
title: Grade 3 Text Evidence Lesson
lessonExplanation: Text evidence is the detail, sentence, event, or quotation from the passage that proves an answer. Grade 3 target: Find a sentence or detail that answers a question. Text complexity: Short passage with explicit evidence. Evidence demand: Use one directly stated detail. Reasoning: Match evidence to an answer.
workedExample: For the answer 'mulch helped,' the best evidence is 'the soil stayed damp longer.'

standardLabel: Describe relationships between events, ideas, and concepts
title: Grade 3 Cause and Effect Lesson
lessonExplanation: A cause tells why something happened. An effect tells what happened. Ask, What happened? and Why did it happen?
workedExample: Because the rain filled the barrel, the class had water for the garden. The rain is the cause, and having water is the effect.

choices: because
finally
both
near
guidedPractice.passage: A school compost bin can turn fruit peels and leaves into rich soil. Students add plant scraps, mix the pile, and keep it slightly damp. Over time, tiny living things break down the scraps. The soil can help a garden grow.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which word often signals a cause?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: Because often explains why something happened.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: because

standardLabel: Identify author's purpose and point of view clues
title: Grade 3 Author's Purpose Lesson
lessonExplanation: Author's purpose is why the author wrote the text. Common purposes are to inform, explain, persuade, or entertain.
workedExample: A text that gives facts about how bees help flowers is written mostly to inform or explain.

choices: to explain how to do something
to tell a fantasy story
to convince readers to move
to describe a character's feelings
guidedPractice.passage: A school compost bin can turn fruit peels and leaves into rich soil. Students add plant scraps, mix the pile, and keep it slightly damp. Over time, tiny living things break down the scraps. The soil can help a garden grow.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: A passage gives steps for making a bird feeder. What is the author's main purpose?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: Steps and directions usually show that the author is explaining a process.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: to explain how to do something

standardLabel: Use text features to locate and interpret information
title: Grade 3 Text Features Lesson
lessonExplanation: Text features help readers find and understand information. Headings, captions, maps, diagrams, labels, and bold words all give clues.
workedExample: A diagram of a plant with labels can show the parts of the plant faster than a paragraph alone.

choices: headings
quotation marks
periods
dialogue
guidedPractice.passage: A school compost bin can turn fruit peels and leaves into rich soil. Students add plant scraps, mix the pile, and keep it slightly damp. Over time, tiny living things break down the scraps. The soil can help a garden grow.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which text feature would best help a reader find the topic of each section?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: Headings tell what a section is mostly about.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: headings

choices: headings

standardLabel: Determine word meaning using context
title: Grade 3 Context Clues Lesson
lessonExplanation: Context clues are words and sentences around an unfamiliar word that help you figure out its meaning.
workedExample: The sentence 'The trail was narrow, so only one hiker could walk through at a time' shows that narrow means not wide.

choices: shy
loud
huge
quick
guidedPractice.passage: The class found a smooth stone beside the stream. Later, they carefully wrote notes about the shape, color, and size of each object they collected.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: In 'The puppy was timid and hid behind the chair,' what does timid most likely mean?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: Hiding behind the chair is a clue that timid means shy.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: shy

standardLabel: Use word parts to determine meaning
title: Grade 3 Prefixes and Suffixes Lesson
lessonExplanation: Prefixes come at the beginning of words. Suffixes come at the end. These word parts can change the meaning of a base word.
workedExample: The prefix re- means again. Reread means read again.

choices: not happy
happy again
very happy
full of happy

guidedPractice.question: Guided: What does unhappy mean?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: The prefix un- often means not.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: not happy

standardLabel: Use word relationships to determine meaning
title: Grade 3 Synonyms and Antonyms Lesson
lessonExplanation: Synonyms have similar meanings. Antonyms have opposite meanings. Use the sentence to choose the word relationship that fits.
workedExample: Tiny and small are synonyms. Tiny and huge are antonyms.

choices: new
old
past
early
guidedPractice.passage: The class found a smooth stone beside the stream. Later, they carefully wrote notes about the shape, color, and size of each object they collected.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which word is an antonym for ancient?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: Ancient means very old, so new is an opposite.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: new

standardLabel: Compare and contrast important points and details
title: Grade 3 Compare and Contrast Lesson
lessonExplanation: Compare means tell how things are alike. Contrast means tell how things are different. Signal words like both, same, however, and unlike can help.
workedExample: Frogs and toads both hatch from eggs, but frogs usually have smoother skin. That sentence compares and contrasts.

choices: unlike the first animal
both animals
in the same way
also has
guidedPractice.passage: A school compost bin can turn fruit peels and leaves into rich soil. Students add plant scraps, mix the pile, and keep it slightly damp. Over time, tiny living things break down the scraps. The soil can help a garden grow.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which phrase signals a contrast?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: Unlike signals a difference between two things.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: unlike the first animal

choices: unlike the first animal
both animals

standardLabel: Determine theme, message, or lesson
title: Grade 3 Theme Lesson
lessonExplanation: Theme is the message or lesson a story suggests about life. A theme is usually a complete idea, not one word. Grade 3 target: Identify the lesson or message in a story. Text complexity: Short story with a clear problem and solution. Evidence demand: Use character actions and ending. Reasoning: State a complete lesson, not one word.
workedExample: If students keep trying until the garden improves, a theme could be: paying attention to a problem helps people solve it.

choices: Paying attention to a problem can help people find a solution.
Gardens are always easy to grow.
Plants do not need care.
Working slowly is never useful.
guidedPractice.passage: The class garden needed more water. Students carried small buckets each morning. Soon they noticed that the soil stayed dry by lunch. They added mulch around the plants, and the soil stayed damp longer. The plants grew stronger during the next week.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Identify the lesson or message in a story.
guidedPractice.coachHint: Use character actions and ending.
guidedPractice.explanation: The best answer is "Paying attention to a problem can help people find a solution." because it meets this Grade 3 expectation: Choose the theme and one supporting event.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Paying attention to a problem can help people find a solution.

standardLabel: Describe characters and explain how actions affect events
title: Grade 3 Character Traits and Actions Lesson
lessonExplanation: A character trait describes what a character is like on the inside. Use actions, words, thoughts, and choices as evidence.
workedExample: If a character keeps practicing after making mistakes, the character may be determined. The repeated practice is the evidence.

choices: Lena gives half her markers to a new student.
Lena sharpens her pencil.
Lena sits near the window.
Lena reads the title.
guidedPractice.passage: Nora wanted to build the tallest block tower in the room. When it fell twice, she took a breath, sorted the blocks by size, and tried again. Her friend Eli held the base steady. This time, the tower stood until cleanup.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which evidence best supports the trait generous?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: Sharing supplies with someone else supports the trait generous.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Lena gives half her markers to a new student.

standardLabel: Describe how story events build in sequence
title: Grade 3 Sequence of Events Lesson
lessonExplanation: Sequence means the order in which events happen. Look for time words and cause-and-effect clues to follow the story.
workedExample: First the class plants seeds, next they water them, and later sprouts appear. The order helps readers understand what caused the change.

choices: after
because
unlike
however
guidedPractice.passage: Nora wanted to build the tallest block tower in the room. When it fell twice, she took a breath, sorted the blocks by size, and tried again. Her friend Eli held the base steady. This time, the tower stood until cleanup.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which word often signals sequence?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: After tells when one event happens compared with another event.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: after

standardLabel: Describe characters, setting, and events
title: Grade 3 Story Elements Lesson
lessonExplanation: Story elements include characters, setting, problem, important events, and solution. Track these parts to understand how a story works.
workedExample: If Maya forgets her lunch, asks for help, and solves the problem by sharing with a friend, the problem is forgetting lunch and the solution is sharing.

choices: The character loses the class pet.
The story happens in spring.
The character likes blue.
The story has four paragraphs.
guidedPractice.passage: Nora wanted to build the tallest block tower in the room. When it fell twice, she took a breath, sorted the blocks by size, and tried again. Her friend Eli held the base steady. This time, the tower stood until cleanup.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which detail best names the problem in a story?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: A problem is something the character must solve or respond to.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: The character loses the class pet.

choices: The character loses the class pet.
The story happens in spring.
The character likes blue.
The story has four paragraphs.

standardLabel: Explain point of view and narrator perspective
title: Grade 3 Point of View Lesson
lessonExplanation: Point of view is who is telling the story or how an author sees a topic. Grade 3 target: Identify who is telling the story. Text complexity: Short story with clear first- or third-person pronouns. Evidence demand: Use pronoun clues. Reasoning: Explain who knows or tells the events.
workedExample: If the text says 'students noticed' and 'they added mulch,' the narrator is outside the story and tells about the class.

choices: The narrator tells about the class as problem solvers.
The narrator is one plant in the garden.
The narrator says the class failed.
The narrator dislikes all science.
guidedPractice.passage: The class garden needed more water. Students carried small buckets each morning. Soon they noticed that the soil stayed dry by lunch. They added mulch around the plants, and the soil stayed damp longer. The plants grew stronger during the next week.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Identify who is telling the story.
guidedPractice.coachHint: Use pronoun clues.
guidedPractice.explanation: The best answer is "The narrator tells about the class as problem solvers." because it meets this Grade 3 expectation: Identify narrator point of view.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: The narrator tells about the class as problem solvers.

standardLabel: Refer to parts of stories, poems, and dramas
title: Grade 3 Poetry Lines and Stanzas Lesson
lessonExplanation: Poems are often written in lines and stanzas. A stanza is a group of lines, like a paragraph in a poem.
workedExample: If a poem has four lines, a space, and four more lines, it has two stanzas.

choices: a group of lines in a poem
the title of a story
a fact in an article
a punctuation mark
guidedPractice.passage: Nora wanted to build the tallest block tower in the room. When it fell twice, she took a breath, sorted the blocks by size, and tried again. Her friend Eli held the base steady. This time, the tower stood until cleanup.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: What is a stanza?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: A stanza groups poem lines together.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: a group of lines in a poem

standardLabel: Determine literal and nonliteral meanings
title: Grade 3 Connotation and Figurative Language Lesson
lessonExplanation: Connotation and figurative language shape meaning. Look for words or phrases that suggest feelings, images, or nonliteral ideas, then explain their effect. Grade 3 target: Recognize literal and nonliteral language. Text complexity: Short sentences with common idioms or simple comparisons. Evidence demand: Use context clues. Reasoning: Explain what the phrase really means.
workedExample: If an author calls a hallway "a river of students," the phrase does not mean real water. It suggests many students moving quickly together. Grade 3 expectation: Choose the meaning of a nonliteral phrase.

choices: It suggests many students were moving together.
It means water filled the hallway.
It means students were fish.
It means the hallway was empty.
guidedPractice.passage: The class garden needed more water. Students carried small buckets each morning. Soon they noticed that the soil stayed dry by lunch. They added mulch around the plants, and the soil stayed damp longer. The plants grew stronger during the next week.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Recognize literal and nonliteral language.
guidedPractice.coachHint: Use context clues.
guidedPractice.explanation: The best answer is "It suggests many students were moving together." because it meets this Grade 3 expectation: Choose the meaning of a nonliteral phrase.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: It suggests many students were moving together.

independentPractice.explanation: The best answer is "It suggests many students were moving together." because it meets this Grade 3 expectation: Choose the meaning of a nonliteral phrase.
independentPractice.correctAnswer: It suggests many students were moving together.

choices: It suggests many students were moving together.
It means water filled the hallway.
It means students were fish.
It means the hallway was empty.
independentPractice.passage: The class garden needed more water. Students carried small buckets each morning. Soon they noticed that the soil stayed dry by lunch. They added mulch around the plants, and the soil stayed damp longer. The plants grew stronger during the next week.

standardLabel: Develop writing with organization and details
title: Grade 3 Paragraph Organization Lesson
lessonExplanation: A clear paragraph has one main idea, details that stay on topic, and an ending sentence that closes the thought.
workedExample: A paragraph about saving water should include details about water use, not a random detail about lunch.

choices: Recycling paper can help save trees.
My favorite lunch is pizza.
The playground is near the gym.
I bought new shoes.
guidedPractice.passage: A good response stays focused on the prompt. It gives a clear idea, includes details from the text, and explains why those details matter.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which sentence best stays on topic in a paragraph about recycling?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: The sentence stays focused on recycling.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Recycling paper can help save trees.

standardLabel: Use capitalization and title conventions
title: Grade 3 Capitalization and Titles Lesson
lessonExplanation: Capitalize names, places, the first word in a sentence, and important words in titles.
workedExample: The title The Garden Journal capitalizes the important words in the title.

choices: The Secret Map
the secret map
The secret map
the Secret map
guidedPractice.passage: The students revised a paragraph about a class project. They checked each sentence for clear meaning, correct punctuation, and words that matched the formal style of the report.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which title is capitalized correctly?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: The important words in the title begin with capital letters.

standardLabel: Use commas and punctuation correctly
title: Grade 3 Commas in a Series Lesson
lessonExplanation: Use commas to separate three or more words or phrases in a series.
workedExample: The class collected leaves, seeds, and twigs. The commas separate the items in the list.

guidedPractice.explanation: The commas separate the three items in the list.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: We packed paper, pencils, and glue.

standardLabel: Demonstrate command of sentence conventions
title: Grade 3 Complete Sentences Lesson
lessonExplanation: A complete sentence has a subject and a predicate and expresses a complete thought.
workedExample: The class built a model is complete. Built a model is incomplete because it does not tell who did the action.

choices: The students measured the plant.
Measured the plant.
After the bell.
In the bright room.
guidedPractice.passage: The students revised a paragraph about a class project. They checked each sentence for clear meaning, correct punctuation, and words that matched the formal style of the report.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which choice is a complete sentence?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: This sentence tells who did something and what they did.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: The students measured the plant.

lessonExplanation: Conventions questions ask you to make writing clear and correct. Read the whole paragraph first, then check pronouns, verb tense, punctuation, and style in context.
workedExample: If a sentence says, "A writer should reread the draft because you may notice missing evidence," the pronoun shifts from "a writer" to "you." A better revision keeps the same person: "A writer should reread the draft because the writer may notice missing evidence."

choices: Sentence 2 should be revised to maintain formal style.

standardLabel: Support opinion writing with reasons
title: Grade 3 Opinion Reasons Lesson
lessonExplanation: Opinion writing states what the writer thinks and gives reasons that support the opinion.
workedExample: Opinion: The class should visit the nature center. Reason: Students can observe animals and plants they are studying.

choices: The park is the best place for the trip because students can learn outside.
The park has trees.
Yesterday was sunny.
The bus is yellow.
guidedPractice.passage: A good response stays focused on the prompt. It gives a clear idea, includes details from the text, and explains why those details matter.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which sentence gives a reason for an opinion?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: The word because introduces a reason that supports the opinion.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: The park is the best place for the trip because students can learn outside.

standardLabel: Use evidence and explanation in text-dependent writing
title: Grade 3 TDA Evidence and Explanation Lesson
lessonExplanation: A strong TDA does not retell the passage. It answers the prompt with a clear claim, uses evidence from the text, and explains how the evidence proves the claim.
workedExample: Claim: The character learns to be more responsible. Evidence: The character returns to fix the mistake without being asked. Explanation: This detail matters because the character chooses responsibility even when no one is forcing the choice.

choices: Add a sentence that explains how the evidence proves the claim.
Copy a longer sentence from the passage.
Start a new paragraph with no evidence.
Replace the claim with a summary of the story.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which revision best teaches a Grade 3 writer to explain evidence instead of just listing it?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Use because to connect the evidence back to the claim.
guidedPractice.explanation: A TDA needs evidence and analysis. The explanation shows why the evidence matters.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Add a sentence that explains how the evidence proves the claim.

standardLabel: Combine main ideas and details across texts
title: Grade 4 Combine Main Ideas Across Texts Lesson
lessonExplanation: When two texts are about related topics, compare the main idea of each text. Then decide what bigger idea both texts help explain.
workedExample: One text may explain how wetlands protect animals, while another explains how wetlands reduce flooding. Together, the texts show that wetlands help both wildlife and communities.

choices: Both texts explain ways students can help the environment.
Both texts are mostly about lunch menus.
One text is fiction and one has no facts.
The texts disagree about planting seeds.
guidedPractice.passage: A school compost bin can turn fruit peels and leaves into rich soil. Students add plant scraps, mix the pile, and keep it slightly damp. Over time, tiny living things break down the scraps. The soil can help a garden grow.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which answer best combines the main ideas of two texts about school gardens and composting?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: The answer combines the shared environmental idea from both topics.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Both texts explain ways students can help the environment.

standardLabel: Determine main idea and explain key details
title: Grade 4 Main Idea Lesson
lessonExplanation: Main idea is what the paragraph, section, or whole passage is mostly about. Grade 4 target: Determine the main idea of a longer section or passage. Text complexity: Several related paragraphs with headings or connected details. Evidence demand: Explain how multiple details support the main idea. Reasoning: Separate important details from smaller examples.
workedExample: If one section describes mulch and another describes morning watering, the main idea should include both tested solutions, not just one detail.

choices: Students compared two ways to help the garden use water wisely.
The students only watered plants in the afternoon.
The garden did not need observations.
Mulch and morning watering are unrelated.
guidedPractice.passage: The fourth-grade science club wanted the school garden to use less water. First, students recorded how dry the soil felt each afternoon. Then they compared two solutions: adding mulch and watering earlier in the morning. After two weeks, the notes showed that mulch kept the soil damp longer, while morning watering helped on very hot days.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Determine the main idea of a longer section or passage.
guidedPractice.coachHint: Explain how multiple details support the main idea.
guidedPractice.explanation: The best answer is "Students compared two ways to help the garden use water wisely." because it meets this Grade 4 expectation: State the main idea and explain how two details support it.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Students compared two ways to help the garden use water wisely.

choices: Students compared two ways to help the garden use water wisely.

standardLabel: Make inferences using text evidence
title: Grade 4 Inference Lesson
lessonExplanation: Inference means using text clues plus what makes sense to figure out an idea the author does not state directly. Grade 4 target: Make an inference and support it with details. Text complexity: Longer passage with two clues leading to one idea. Evidence demand: Use at least two details. Reasoning: Explain why the clues support the inference.
workedExample: If students recorded soil data before choosing a solution, you can infer they wanted evidence before deciding.

choices: The students used observations before deciding which solution worked best.
The students guessed without collecting information.
The students stopped the project after one day.
The garden did not change.
guidedPractice.passage: The fourth-grade science club wanted the school garden to use less water. First, students recorded how dry the soil felt each afternoon. Then they compared two solutions: adding mulch and watering earlier in the morning. After two weeks, the notes showed that mulch kept the soil damp longer, while morning watering helped on very hot days.

standardLabel: Make inferences and support them with details
title: Grade 4 Inference with Supporting Details Lesson
lessonExplanation: A strong inference is supported by more than one detail. Use clues from the text, then choose evidence that proves the idea.
workedExample: If a student checks measurements twice and fixes a chart before sharing it, you can infer the student wants the work to be accurate.

choices: The class cares about accuracy.
The class wants to avoid science.
The class finished without working.
The class copied another project.
guidedPractice.passage: A school compost bin can turn fruit peels and leaves into rich soil. Students add plant scraps, mix the pile, and keep it slightly damp. Over time, tiny living things break down the scraps. The soil can help a garden grow.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which inference is best supported by details about a class checking data twice?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: Checking data twice supports the idea that accuracy matters to the class.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: The class cares about accuracy.

standardLabel: Cite relevant details and examples from text
title: Grade 4 Text Evidence Lesson
lessonExplanation: Text evidence is the detail, sentence, event, or quotation from the passage that proves an answer. Grade 4 target: Use details and examples to support an answer. Text complexity: Longer passage with related details. Evidence demand: Use relevant details, not just nearby sentences. Reasoning: Explain how the detail supports the answer.
workedExample: For the claim that students compared solutions, cite the note that mulch kept soil damp and morning watering helped on hot days.

Then they compared two solutions: adding mulch and watering earlier in the morning. After two weeks, the notes showed that mulch kept the soil damp longer, while morning watering helped on very hot days.
masteryCheck.question: Mastery: Use details and examples to support an answer.
masteryCheck.coachHint: Use relevant details, not just nearby sentences.
masteryCheck.explanation: The best answer is "The notes showed that mulch kept soil damp longer and morning watering helped on hot days." because it meets this Grade 4 expectation: Select evidence and explain its connection.
masteryCheck.correctAnswer: The notes showed that mulch kept soil damp longer and morning watering helped on hot days.

standardLabel: Compare accounts and explain author's purpose
title: Grade 4 Author's Purpose and Message Lesson
lessonExplanation: Author's purpose is why the text was written. The author's message is the idea the author wants readers to understand or believe.
workedExample: A text about saving local streams may inform readers about pollution and also send the message that small actions can protect water.

choices: Students can make a positive difference at school.
Lunch should always be shorter.
Recycling is impossible.
Only adults solve school problems.
guidedPractice.passage: A school compost bin can turn fruit peels and leaves into rich soil. Students add plant scraps, mix the pile, and keep it slightly damp. Over time, tiny living things break down the scraps. The soil can help a garden grow.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: A passage explains how students reduced cafeteria waste. What is the author's likely message?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: The message connects the students' actions to a positive result.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Students can make a positive difference at school.

standardLabel: Describe text structure and organization
title: Grade 4 Problem and Solution Text Structure Lesson
lessonExplanation: In problem and solution structure, the author explains an issue and one or more ways to fix it. Signal words include problem, solve, challenge, and solution.
workedExample: If a garden dries out quickly and students install mulch to keep moisture in the soil, the dry soil is the problem and mulch is part of the solution.

choices: The path flooded, so workers added a drain.
The path is beside a field.
The path opened in June.
The path has signs and benches.
guidedPractice.passage: A school compost bin can turn fruit peels and leaves into rich soil. Students add plant scraps, mix the pile, and keep it slightly damp. Over time, tiny living things break down the scraps. The soil can help a garden grow.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which pair shows problem and solution?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: Flooding is the problem, and adding a drain is the solution.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: The path flooded, so workers added a drain.

standardLabel: Use reference materials to clarify meaning

workedExample: If you need a stronger word than good, a thesaurus may suggest helpful words like excellent or useful, depending on the sentence.

standardLabel: Use roots and affixes to determine word meaning
title: Grade 4 Greek and Latin Roots Lesson
lessonExplanation: Greek and Latin roots are word parts that carry meaning. Knowing roots helps you figure out unfamiliar words.
workedExample: The root aqua means water. An aquarium is a place for water animals or plants.

choices: to carry across or move
to write again
to look closely
to make smaller
guidedPractice.passage: The class found a smooth stone beside the stream. Later, they carefully wrote notes about the shape, color, and size of each object they collected.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: If port means carry, what does transport most likely mean?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: The root port gives a clue about carrying or moving something.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: to carry across or move

standardLabel: Use context to determine word meaning
title: Grade 4 Homophones and Multiple-Meaning Words Lesson
lessonExplanation: Homophones sound alike but have different meanings. Multiple-meaning words have more than one meaning. Use context to choose the correct meaning.
workedExample: In 'The team will present the report,' present means show or share. In 'I opened the present,' it means a gift.

choices: their
there
they're
thare
guidedPractice.passage: The class found a smooth stone beside the stream. Later, they carefully wrote notes about the shape, color, and size of each object they collected.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which word correctly completes the sentence: The class read _____ reports aloud.
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: Their shows ownership: the reports belong to the class.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: their

standardLabel: Explain reasons and evidence supporting points
title: Grade 4 Argumentative Text Clues Lesson
lessonExplanation: Argumentative texts make a claim and support it with reasons and evidence. Look for opinion words, reasons, facts, and calls to action.
workedExample: The sentence 'Our school should add a recycling team because it would reduce waste' includes a claim and a reason.

choices: Our town should build more bike paths.
The path is two miles long.
Many students own bikes.
The meeting begins at six.
guidedPractice.passage: A school compost bin can turn fruit peels and leaves into rich soil. Students add plant scraps, mix the pile, and keep it slightly damp. Over time, tiny living things break down the scraps. The soil can help a garden grow.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which sentence is a claim?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: A claim states a position that reasons can support.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Our town should build more bike paths.

choices: Our town should build more bike paths.

standardLabel: Determine theme and summarize key details
title: Grade 4 Theme Lesson
lessonExplanation: Theme is the message or lesson a story suggests about life. A theme is usually a complete idea, not one word. Grade 4 target: Determine theme using key story details. Text complexity: Story with character change or repeated lesson clues. Evidence demand: Use events and character response. Reasoning: Explain how events reveal the theme.
workedExample: If students test more than one solution, a theme could be: careful testing leads to better decisions.

choices: Careful testing can lead to better choices.
Only one idea should ever be tried.
Notes do not help solve problems.
Hot days are not important.
guidedPractice.passage: The fourth-grade science club wanted the school garden to use less water. First, students recorded how dry the soil felt each afternoon. Then they compared two solutions: adding mulch and watering earlier in the morning. After two weeks, the notes showed that mulch kept the soil damp longer, while morning watering helped on very hot days.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Determine theme using key story details.
guidedPractice.coachHint: Use events and character response.
guidedPractice.explanation: The best answer is "Careful testing can lead to better choices." because it meets this Grade 4 expectation: Support the theme with details from the story.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Careful testing can lead to better choices.

standardLabel: Describe characters in depth using text evidence
title: Grade 4 Compare Characters Lesson
lessonExplanation: To compare characters, look at their actions, words, thoughts, and choices. Strong comparisons use evidence for both characters.
workedExample: If one character rushes into a plan and another asks questions first, they differ in how carefully they make decisions.

choices: Ari acts quickly, but Mina studies the problem first.
Ari wears a blue jacket.
Mina stands near the door.
The story has four scenes.
guidedPractice.passage: Nora wanted to build the tallest block tower in the room. When it fell twice, she took a breath, sorted the blocks by size, and tried again. Her friend Eli held the base steady. This time, the tower stood until cleanup.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which detail best compares two characters?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: This choice compares how both characters respond to a problem.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Ari acts quickly, but Mina studies the problem first.

standardLabel: Describe plot events and character responses
title: Grade 4 Plot Elements Lesson
lessonExplanation: Plot includes the problem, rising events, climax, resolution, and how characters respond. Analyze how events connect instead of listing them separately.
workedExample: When a lost map causes characters to work together, that event pushes the plot forward and changes how the characters behave.

choices: The team discovers the missing key inside the toolbox.
The room has a window.
The clock is round.
The character likes green.
guidedPractice.passage: Nora wanted to build the tallest block tower in the room. When it fell twice, she took a breath, sorted the blocks by size, and tried again. Her friend Eli held the base steady. This time, the tower stood until cleanup.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which detail most likely moves the plot forward?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: Finding the key can cause new events and help solve the problem.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: The team discovers the missing key inside the toolbox.

standardLabel: Compare points of view and perspectives
title: Grade 4 Point of View Lesson
lessonExplanation: Point of view is who is telling the story or how an author sees a topic. Grade 4 target: Compare first- and third-person narration. Text complexity: Story excerpts with different narrators. Evidence demand: Use pronouns and narrator knowledge. Reasoning: Explain how narration changes what readers know.
workedExample: The narrator emphasizes observations and notes, which presents the students as careful investigators.

choices: The narrator presents the students as careful investigators.
The narrator makes fun of the students.
The narrator hides the results.
The narrator says observations are useless.
guidedPractice.passage: The fourth-grade science club wanted the school garden to use less water. First, students recorded how dry the soil felt each afternoon. Then they compared two solutions: adding mulch and watering earlier in the morning. After two weeks, the notes showed that mulch kept the soil damp longer, while morning watering helped on very hot days.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Compare first- and third-person narration.
guidedPractice.coachHint: Use pronouns and narrator knowledge.
guidedPractice.explanation: The best answer is "The narrator presents the students as careful investigators." because it meets this Grade 4 expectation: Compare narrator perspective using evidence.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: The narrator presents the students as careful investigators.

standardLabel: Explain structural elements of poems
title: Grade 4 Rhyme Scheme and Poetry Elements Lesson
lessonExplanation: Poetry elements include lines, stanzas, rhythm, rhyme, and repeated sounds. Rhyme scheme is the pattern of end rhymes in a poem.
workedExample: If line 1 rhymes with line 3 and line 2 rhymes with line 4, the pattern can be labeled ABAB.

choices: the pattern of end rhymes
the main character
the setting of an article
the number of facts in a paragraph
guidedPractice.passage: Nora wanted to build the tallest block tower in the room. When it fell twice, she took a breath, sorted the blocks by size, and tried again. Her friend Eli held the base steady. This time, the tower stood until cleanup.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: What does rhyme scheme describe?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: Rhyme scheme names how the ending sounds of poem lines match.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: the pattern of end rhymes

standardLabel: Determine meaning of words, phrases, and references
title: Grade 4 Allusion Meaning Lesson
lessonExplanation: An allusion is a reference to a known story, person, event, or idea. Use context and background clues to understand what the reference adds.
workedExample: Calling a difficult task 'a mountain to climb' alludes to a hard journey and suggests the task will take effort.

choices: The helper was very useful in a difficult moment.
The helper worked at a pool.
The helper carried a boat.
The helper told a joke.
guidedPractice.passage: The class found a smooth stone beside the stream. Later, they carefully wrote notes about the shape, color, and size of each object they collected.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: If a narrator says a helper was 'a real lifesaver,' what does the phrase suggest?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: The phrase suggests the helper made a difficult situation much better.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: The helper was very useful in a difficult moment.

standardLabel: Interpret figurative language in context
title: Grade 4 Connotation and Figurative Language Lesson
lessonExplanation: Connotation and figurative language shape meaning. Look for words or phrases that suggest feelings, images, or nonliteral ideas, then explain their effect. Grade 4 target: Interpret similes, metaphors, and word meaning. Text complexity: Short passage with figurative phrases in context. Evidence demand: Use surrounding details. Reasoning: Explain the image or comparison.
workedExample: If an author calls a hallway "a river of students," the phrase does not mean real water. It suggests many students moving quickly together. Grade 4 expectation: Explain what figurative language means in context.

choices: It creates an image of busy movement.
It proves the hallway was outside.
It gives a literal measurement.
It changes the setting to a river.
guidedPractice.passage: The fourth-grade science club wanted the school garden to use less water. First, students recorded how dry the soil felt each afternoon. Then they compared two solutions: adding mulch and watering earlier in the morning. After two weeks, the notes showed that mulch kept the soil damp longer, while morning watering helped on very hot days.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Interpret similes, metaphors, and word meaning.
guidedPractice.coachHint: Use surrounding details.
guidedPractice.explanation: The best answer is "It creates an image of busy movement." because it meets this Grade 4 expectation: Explain what figurative language means in context.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: It creates an image of busy movement.

masteryCheck.explanation: The best answer is "It creates an image of busy movement." because it meets this Grade 4 expectation: Explain what figurative language means in context.
masteryCheck.correctAnswer: It creates an image of busy movement.

standardLabel: Determine meaning and effect of words and phrases
title: Grade 4 Sensory Details Lesson
lessonExplanation: Sensory details help readers see, hear, smell, taste, or feel what is happening. They make writing more vivid and specific.
workedExample: The sentence 'The dry leaves crackled under my shoes' uses sound to help the reader imagine the scene.

choices: The cinnamon smell drifted from the kitchen.
The recipe had four steps.
The kitchen was in the house.
The plate was round.
guidedPractice.passage: Nora wanted to build the tallest block tower in the room. When it fell twice, she took a breath, sorted the blocks by size, and tried again. Her friend Eli held the base steady. This time, the tower stood until cleanup.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which sentence uses a sensory detail?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: The sentence uses smell to create a clear image.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: The cinnamon smell drifted from the kitchen.

standardLabel: Interpret similes, metaphors, and figurative language
title: Grade 4 Similes and Metaphors Lesson
lessonExplanation: Similes compare using like or as. Metaphors compare by saying one thing is another. Both help readers understand an idea in a fresh way.
workedExample: The classroom was a beehive means the classroom was busy, not that it was really full of bees.

choices: Many students were moving through the hallway.
Water filled the hallway.
Students were swimming.
The hallway was empty.
guidedPractice.passage: The class found a smooth stone beside the stream. Later, they carefully wrote notes about the shape, color, and size of each object they collected.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: What does the metaphor 'the hallway was a river of students' suggest?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: The metaphor compares moving students to flowing water.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Many students were moving through the hallway.

standardLabel: Write clear summaries with relevant details
title: Grade 4 Summarizing Stories Lesson
lessonExplanation: A strong summary includes the most important events and leaves out tiny details or personal opinions.
workedExample: Instead of listing every line of dialogue, summarize the character's problem, key actions, and solution.

choices: A girl solves the problem by asking her neighbors for help.
The girl's shoes are blue.
I liked the story a lot.
The second paragraph has six sentences.
guidedPractice.passage: A good response stays focused on the prompt. It gives a clear idea, includes details from the text, and explains why those details matter.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which sentence belongs in a summary?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: This sentence includes an important event and solution.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: A girl solves the problem by asking her neighbors for help.

standardLabel: Organize writing with linking words and transitions
title: Grade 4 Transitions and Linking Words Lesson
lessonExplanation: Transitions connect ideas and help readers follow the order, cause, contrast, or addition of information.
workedExample: Words like first, next, because, however, and as a result show how ideas are related.

choices: as a result
nearby
for example
before
guidedPractice.passage: A good response stays focused on the prompt. It gives a clear idea, includes details from the text, and explains why those details matter.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which transition best shows a result?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: As a result signals that one event or idea caused another.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: as a result

standardLabel: Demonstrate command of spelling and conventions
title: Grade 4 Spelling with Affixes Lesson
lessonExplanation: Affixes are prefixes and suffixes. When adding suffixes, check whether the base word changes before the ending is added.
workedExample: Hope becomes hopeful when -ful is added. Care becomes careless when -less is added.

choices: careless
careles
carlesss
careluss
guidedPractice.passage: The students revised a paragraph about a class project. They checked each sentence for clear meaning, correct punctuation, and words that matched the formal style of the report.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which word is spelled correctly?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: Careless correctly combines care and -less.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: careless

standardLabel: Support opinion writing with reasons and evidence
title: Grade 4 Supporting Reasons Lesson
lessonExplanation: A reason explains why a claim or opinion makes sense. Strong reasons are specific and connected to the claim.
workedExample: Claim: Students should have more outdoor reading time. Reason: Reading outside can make quiet independent reading feel fresh and focused.

choices: Students could observe exhibits that connect to their science unit.
The bus has windows.
The museum has a front door.
The trip is on Tuesday.
guidedPractice.passage: A good response stays focused on the prompt. It gives a clear idea, includes details from the text, and explains why those details matter.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which reason best supports the claim that a class should visit a science museum?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: The reason explains an educational benefit connected to the claim.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Students could observe exhibits that connect to their science unit.

standardLabel: Use facts and details to develop writing
title: Grade 4 Facts and Opinions Lesson
lessonExplanation: A fact can be proven true or false. An opinion tells what someone thinks, feels, or believes.
workedExample: The library opens at 9:00 is a fact. The library is the best place in town is an opinion.

choices: The new mural is the most beautiful part of the school.
The mural is six feet tall.
The mural has blue paint.
The mural was finished on Friday.
guidedPractice.passage: A good response stays focused on the prompt. It gives a clear idea, includes details from the text, and explains why those details matter.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which sentence is an opinion?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: Most beautiful is a judgment that people could disagree about.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: The new mural is the most beautiful part of the school.

standardLabel: Use evidence and explanation in text-dependent writing
title: Grade 4 TDA Evidence and Explanation Lesson
lessonExplanation: A strong TDA does not retell the passage. It answers the prompt with a clear claim, uses evidence from the text, and explains how the evidence proves the claim.
workedExample: Claim: The character learns to be more responsible. Evidence: The character returns to fix the mistake without being asked. Explanation: This detail matters because the character chooses responsibility even when no one is forcing the choice.

choices: Add a sentence that explains how the evidence proves the claim.
Copy a longer sentence from the passage.
Start a new paragraph with no evidence.
Replace the claim with a summary of the story.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which revision best teaches a Grade 4 writer to explain evidence instead of just listing it?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Use because to connect the evidence back to the claim.
guidedPractice.explanation: A TDA needs evidence and analysis. The explanation shows why the evidence matters.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Add a sentence that explains how the evidence proves the claim.

standardLabel: Determine main ideas and summarize key details
title: Grade 5 Main Idea Lesson
lessonExplanation: Main idea is what the paragraph, section, or whole passage is mostly about. Grade 5 target: Summarize main ideas across sections or related texts. Text complexity: Longer passage or paired short texts with more than one important section. Evidence demand: Use multiple details from different parts of the text. Reasoning: Synthesize important ideas and leave out minor details.
workedExample: If two related texts describe mulch and rain barrels, a strong summary combines the shared idea: conservation improves when people use more than one water-saving method.

choices: Different conservation methods can work together to protect a garden.
One class studied only rain barrels.
Dry weather makes gardens impossible.
The project was mostly about carrying buckets.
guidedPractice.passage: Two classes studied ways to protect the school garden during dry weather. One group tested mulch and found that covered soil lost less moisture. Another group studied rain barrels and learned that stored rainwater reduced the need for extra hose water. Taken together, the projects showed that conservation works best when students combine daily habits with tools that save resources.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Summarize main ideas across sections or related texts.
guidedPractice.coachHint: Use multiple details from different parts of the text.
guidedPractice.explanation: The best answer is "Different conservation methods can work together to protect a garden." because it meets this Grade 5 expectation: Write or select a summary that combines the central idea with the most relevant details.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Different conservation methods can work together to protect a garden.

standardLabel: Analyze multiple accounts of the same event or topic
skill: Multiple Accounts of the Same Event
title: Grade 5 Multiple Accounts of the Same Event Lesson
lessonExplanation: Different texts can describe the same event in different ways. Compare which details each author emphasizes and how the point of view changes the account.
workedExample: A newspaper article may focus on facts about a storm, while a diary entry may focus on one person's feelings during the storm.

choices: One account focuses on facts, while the other focuses on a student's experience.
Both accounts use periods.
One account has more pages.
Both accounts have the same first word.
guidedPractice.passage: A school compost bin can turn fruit peels and leaves into rich soil. Students add plant scraps, mix the pile, and keep it slightly damp. Over time, tiny living things break down the scraps. The soil can help a garden grow.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which comparison best analyzes two accounts of a school event?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: This choice compares how the accounts present information differently.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: One account focuses on facts, while the other focuses on a student's experience.

independentPractice.passage: A school compost bin can turn fruit peels and leaves into rich soil. Students add plant scraps, mix the pile, and keep it slightly damp. Over time, tiny living things break down the scraps. The soil can help a garden grow.

independentPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
independentPractice.explanation: This choice compares how the accounts present information differently. This item adds another chance to apply the same skill with the Grade 5 evidence expectation.
independentPractice.correctAnswer: One account focuses on facts, while the other focuses on a student's experience.

standardLabel: Use text structure to interpret information
title: Grade 5 Chronology and Sequence Text Structure Lesson
lessonExplanation: Chronological structure presents events in time order. Look for dates, time words, and steps that show how events unfold.
workedExample: A text about building a bridge might explain planning first, construction next, and opening day last.

choices: By 2018, later, finally
however, unlike, but
problem, solution, challenge
for example, such as, including

guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which clue best signals chronological order?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: These words show when events happened.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: By 2018, later, finally

standardLabel: Determine academic and domain-specific word meaning
title: Grade 5 Academic and Domain-Specific Vocabulary Lesson
lessonExplanation: Academic words appear across school subjects. Domain-specific words belong to one topic or field. Use context and word parts to determine meaning.
workedExample: In a science article, evaporation is domain-specific because it belongs to the study of water and matter.

choices: habitat
quickly
however
important

guidedPractice.question: Guided: In an article about ecosystems, which word is domain-specific?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: Habitat is a science word connected to ecosystems.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: habitat

standardLabel: Use word relationships to deepen understanding
title: Grade 5 Analogies and Word Relationships Lesson
lessonExplanation: Analogies show relationships between words. Identify the relationship in the first pair, then apply it to the second pair.
workedExample: Seed is to plant as egg is to bird. The relationship is beginning stage to grown living thing.

chapter
sentence

guidedPractice.explanation: An author creates a book; a painter creates a portrait.

lessonExplanation: Roots and affixes carry meaning. Use them with context clues to figure out unfamiliar words.
workedExample: The root scrib means write. A manuscript is something written by hand or prepared as writing.

standardLabel: Explain how reasons and evidence support points
title: Grade 5 Reasons and Evidence in Arguments Lesson
lessonExplanation: An argument makes a claim and supports it with reasons and evidence. Strong evidence is relevant, specific, and connected to the claim.
workedExample: Claim: The school should add water bottle stations. Evidence: Students used 400 fewer plastic bottles at a nearby school after stations were installed.

choices: Trees can provide shade and lower the temperature on hot days.
The playground has swings.
Trees have leaves.
Students arrive at 8:00.
guidedPractice.passage: A school compost bin can turn fruit peels and leaves into rich soil. Students add plant scraps, mix the pile, and keep it slightly damp. Over time, tiny living things break down the scraps. The soil can help a garden grow.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which evidence best supports the claim that students should plant trees near the playground?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: This evidence explains a direct benefit of planting trees.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Trees can provide shade and lower the temperature on hot days.

standardLabel: Integrate information from several texts on the same topic
title: Grade 5 Integrating Information from Sources Lesson
lessonExplanation: To integrate information, combine details from more than one source to answer a question or explain a topic more completely.
workedExample: One source may explain how solar panels work; another may show cost savings. Together, they help explain benefits and challenges.

choices: Both sources show that pollinators help plants grow, and one source explains how gardens can protect them.
One source has a title.
The sources are printed in black ink.
Both sources use the word plant once.

guidedPractice.explanation: This choice combines important information from both sources.

standardLabel: Determine theme and summarize literary text
title: Grade 5 Theme Lesson
lessonExplanation: Theme is the message or lesson a story suggests about life. A theme is usually a complete idea, not one word. Grade 5 target: Compare themes or explain how a theme develops. Text complexity: Story with layered conflict or related stories. Evidence demand: Use events from different parts of the text. Reasoning: Explain how character choices develop the theme.
workedExample: If groups combine what they learned, a theme could be: people make stronger progress when they learn from one another.

choices: People make stronger progress when they combine ideas and learn from evidence.
Teamwork prevents all mistakes.
Tools matter more than people.
Dry weather always wins.
guidedPractice.passage: Two classes studied ways to protect the school garden during dry weather. One group tested mulch and found that covered soil lost less moisture. Another group studied rain barrels and learned that stored rainwater reduced the need for extra hose water. Taken together, the projects showed that conservation works best when students combine daily habits with tools that save resources.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Compare themes or explain how a theme develops.
guidedPractice.coachHint: Use events from different parts of the text.
guidedPractice.explanation: The best answer is "People make stronger progress when they combine ideas and learn from evidence." because it meets this Grade 5 expectation: Explain or compare theme using evidence.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: People make stronger progress when they combine ideas and learn from evidence.

standardLabel: Compare characters, settings, and events using details
title: Grade 5 Character Response and Plot Lesson
lessonExplanation: In Grade 5, plot analysis includes explaining how characters respond to challenges and how those responses move events forward.
workedExample: If a character admits a mistake, that choice may rebuild trust and lead to the story's resolution.

choices: Jalen apologizes and helps repair the damaged project.
Jalen's backpack is red.
The room has four windows.
The story begins on Monday.
guidedPractice.passage: Nora wanted to build the tallest block tower in the room. When it fell twice, she took a breath, sorted the blocks by size, and tried again. Her friend Eli held the base steady. This time, the tower stood until cleanup.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which detail best shows a character response that affects the plot?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: The apology and repair are actions that can change what happens next.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Jalen apologizes and helps repair the damaged project.

standardLabel: Analyze narrator or speaker point of view
title: Grade 5 Point of View Lesson
lessonExplanation: Point of view is who is telling the story or how an author sees a topic. Grade 5 target: Analyze how narrator or speaker point of view affects events. Text complexity: Story or poem with a clear speaker attitude. Evidence demand: Use words, thoughts, and emphasized details. Reasoning: Explain how perspective shapes meaning.
workedExample: The account focuses on evidence from two groups, shaping the reader to see the project as collaborative research.

choices: The account emphasizes student research and cooperation.
The account focuses only on one student's feelings.
The narrator argues conservation is impossible.
The narrator ignores the class results.
guidedPractice.passage: Two classes studied ways to protect the school garden during dry weather. One group tested mulch and found that covered soil lost less moisture. Another group studied rain barrels and learned that stored rainwater reduced the need for extra hose water. Taken together, the projects showed that conservation works best when students combine daily habits with tools that save resources.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Analyze how narrator or speaker point of view affects events.
guidedPractice.coachHint: Use words, thoughts, and emphasized details.
guidedPractice.explanation: The best answer is "The account emphasizes student research and cooperation." because it meets this Grade 5 expectation: Analyze how point of view influences the reader's understanding.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: The account emphasizes student research and cooperation.

standardLabel: Explain how chapters, scenes, or stanzas fit together
title: Grade 5 Story Structure and Chapters Lesson
lessonExplanation: Chapters, scenes, and stanzas organize a text. Ask how each part adds to the conflict, reveals a character, or prepares for the resolution.
workedExample: A chapter that flashes back to an earlier promise may explain why the character makes a difficult choice later.

choices: To explain a character's later decision
To remove the story problem
To list random facts
To avoid describing characters
guidedPractice.passage: Nora wanted to build the tallest block tower in the room. When it fell twice, she took a breath, sorted the blocks by size, and tried again. Her friend Eli held the base steady. This time, the tower stood until cleanup.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Why might an author include a chapter about an earlier event?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: Earlier events can help readers understand later choices.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: To explain a character's later decision

standardLabel: Interpret figurative language and word meaning
title: Grade 5 Connotation and Figurative Language Lesson
lessonExplanation: Connotation and figurative language shape meaning. Look for words or phrases that suggest feelings, images, or nonliteral ideas, then explain their effect. Grade 5 target: Analyze figurative language, tone, and word relationships. Text complexity: Literary or informational passage with purposeful word choice. Evidence demand: Use context and word relationships. Reasoning: Explain how words affect meaning or tone.
workedExample: If an author calls a hallway "a river of students," the phrase does not mean real water. It suggests many students moving quickly together. Grade 5 expectation: Explain the effect of figurative language or word choice.

choices: It emphasizes the crowded, flowing movement of students.
It suggests students were silent.
It removes the image from the sentence.
It shows the speaker is underwater.
guidedPractice.passage: Two classes studied ways to protect the school garden during dry weather. One group tested mulch and found that covered soil lost less moisture. standardLabel: Analyze word choice, tone, and speaker meaning
title: Grade 5 Poetry Speaker and Tone Lesson
lessonExplanation: The speaker is the voice in a poem. Tone is the speaker's attitude. Use word choice and details to identify both.
workedExample: Words like whisper, silver, and still may create a calm or peaceful tone.

choices: trembled, hurried, uncertain
bright, cheerful, simple
smooth, gentle, quiet
steady, proud, clear
guidedPractice.passage: Nora wanted to build the tallest block tower in the room. When it fell twice, she took a breath, sorted the blocks by size, and tried again. Her friend Eli held the base steady. This time, the tower stood until cleanup.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which words would most likely create a worried tone?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: Those words suggest nervousness or worry.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: trembled, hurried, uncertain

standardLabel: Compare stories in the same genre on themes and topics
title: Grade 5 Compare Themes Across Stories Lesson
lessonExplanation: When stories share a topic, compare the lessons or messages each story develops. Use character choices, conflict, and endings as evidence.
workedExample: Two stories about teamwork may have different themes: one may show that teamwork solves problems, while another shows that teamwork requires listening.

choices: Both stories show that trust grows when people listen to one another.
Both stories have titles.
One story is longer than the other.
Both stories mention a school.
guidedPractice.passage: Nora wanted to build the tallest block tower in the room. When it fell twice, she took a breath, sorted the blocks by size, and tried again. Her friend Eli held the base steady. This time, the tower stood until cleanup.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which answer best compares themes across two stories?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: This choice compares a message developed by both stories.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Both stories show that trust grows when people listen to one another.

standardLabel: Write informative texts with facts and details
title: Grade 5 Informative Essay Development Lesson
lessonExplanation: Informative writing explains a topic with facts, definitions, examples, and clear organization.
workedExample: A paragraph about erosion should explain what erosion is, give an example, and show why it matters.

choices: Paper can be recycled into new products such as notebooks and boxes.
Recycling is my favorite word.
The bin is blue and near the wall.
I once saw a truck.
guidedPractice.passage: A good response stays focused on the prompt. It gives a clear idea, includes details from the text, and explains why those details matter.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which detail best develops an informative paragraph about recycling?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: This fact explains the topic with useful information.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Paper can be recycled into new products such as notebooks and boxes.

standardLabel: Use conventions to produce clear sentences
title: Grade 5 Combining Sentences Lesson
lessonExplanation: Combining sentences can make writing smoother. Keep the meaning clear and avoid repeating the same words too often.
workedExample: Instead of 'The class planted seeds. The class watered seeds,' write 'The class planted and watered seeds.'

choices: The trail was steep and rocky.
The trail was steep the trail was rocky.
Steep and the trail rocky.
The trail was and rocky steep.
guidedPractice.passage: The students revised a paragraph about a class project. They checked each sentence for clear meaning, correct punctuation, and words that matched the formal style of the report.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which sentence best combines the ideas? The trail was steep. The trail was rocky.
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: This sentence combines both descriptions clearly.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: The trail was steep and rocky.

standardLabel: Use commas and punctuation correctly
title: Grade 5 Commas and Introductory Elements Lesson
lessonExplanation: Use a comma after an introductory word, phrase, or clause when it helps separate the opening from the main sentence.
workedExample: After the rain stopped, the team returned to the field.

choices: After the bell rang, the students lined up.
After, the bell rang the students lined up.
After the bell, rang the students lined up.
After the bell rang the students, lined up.
guidedPractice.passage: The students revised a paragraph about a class project. They checked each sentence for clear meaning, correct punctuation, and words that matched the formal style of the report.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which sentence uses a comma correctly?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: The comma separates the introductory clause from the main sentence.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: After the bell rang, the students lined up.

choices: After the bell rang, the students lined up.

standardLabel: Conventions of Standard English - Formal and Informal Style
title: Grade 5 Formal and Informal Style Lesson
lessonExplanation: Conventions questions ask you to make writing clear and correct. Read the whole paragraph first, then check pronouns, verb tense, punctuation, and style in context.
workedExample: If a sentence says, "A writer should reread the draft because you may notice missing evidence," the pronoun shifts from "a writer" to "you." A better revision keeps the same person: "A writer should reread the draft because the writer may notice missing evidence."

choices: Sentence 2 should be revised to maintain formal style.
Sentence 1 should remove the word students.
Sentence 3 should change class to classes.
Sentence 4 should use informal language.
guidedPractice.passage: (1) When students revise an essay, they should check whether each detail supports the claim. (2) A writer should reread the draft because you may notice missing evidence. (3) The class discussed its ideas before writing the final paragraph. (4) Clear organization helps readers follow the explanation.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Read the paragraph. Which sentence should be revised?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Check whether the sentence keeps the same person and style from beginning to end.
guidedPractice.explanation: Sentence 2 shifts from a general noun to you. Keeping pronouns consistent makes the writing clearer and more formal.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Sentence 2 should be revised to maintain formal style.

standardLabel: Conventions of Standard English - Pronoun Agreement and Shifts
title: Grade 5 Pronoun Agreement and Shifts Lesson
lessonExplanation: Conventions questions ask you to make writing clear and correct. Read the whole paragraph first, then check pronouns, verb tense, punctuation, and style in context.
workedExample: If a sentence says, "A writer should reread the draft because you may notice missing evidence," the pronoun shifts from "a writer" to "you." A better revision keeps the same person: "A writer should reread the draft because the writer may notice missing evidence."

standardLabel: Maintain consistent verb tense in writing
title: Grade 5 Verb Tense Consistency Lesson
lessonExplanation: Verb tense should stay consistent unless the time changes. Check whether actions happen in the past, present, or future.
workedExample: The team measured the garden and recorded the results keeps both verbs in past tense.

choices: Mia opened the notebook and wrote the results.
Mia opened the notebook and writes the results.
Mia opens the notebook and wrote the results.
Mia will open the notebook and wrote the results.
guidedPractice.passage: The students revised a paragraph about a class project. They checked each sentence for clear meaning, correct punctuation, and words that matched the formal style of the report.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which sentence keeps verb tense consistent?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: Opened and wrote are both past-tense verbs.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Mia opened the notebook and wrote the results.
