standardLabel: Write opinion pieces with reasons and evidence
title: Grade 5 Opinion Essay Organization Lesson
lessonExplanation: An opinion essay states a claim, gives reasons, supports those reasons with evidence, and uses transitions to connect ideas.
workedExample: Claim: Students should read outside once a week. Reason: Outdoor reading can increase focus. Evidence: The class stayed quiet and read longer during the outdoor trial.

choices: Our class should create a student garden.
The garden has soil.
Seeds are small.
Yesterday was warm.
guidedPractice.passage: A good response stays focused on the prompt. It gives a clear idea, includes details from the text, and explains why those details matter.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which sentence would work best as an opinion essay claim?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: The sentence states a position that can be supported with reasons.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Our class should create a student garden.

standardLabel: Use narrative techniques to develop experiences
title: Grade 5 Narrative Techniques Lesson
lessonExplanation: Narrative writing uses dialogue, description, pacing, and sequence to develop events and characters.
workedExample: Dialogue like 'We can fix it if we work together,' said Ana, can reveal character and move the story forward.

choices: 'I found the missing map,' Luis whispered.

standardLabel: Use evidence and explanation in text-dependent writing
title: Grade 5 TDA Evidence and Explanation Lesson
lessonExplanation: A strong TDA does not retell the passage. It answers the prompt with a clear claim, uses evidence from the text, and explains how the evidence proves the claim.
workedExample: Claim: The character learns to be more responsible. Evidence: The character returns to fix the mistake without being asked. Explanation: This detail matters because the character chooses responsibility even when no one is forcing the choice.

choices: Add a sentence that explains how the evidence proves the claim.
Copy a longer sentence from the passage.
Start a new paragraph with no evidence.
Replace the claim with a summary of the story.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which revision best teaches a Grade 5 writer to explain evidence instead of just listing it?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Use because to connect the evidence back to the claim.
guidedPractice.explanation: A TDA needs evidence and analysis. The explanation shows why the evidence matters.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Add a sentence that explains how the evidence proves the claim.

standardLabel: Analyze how a central idea is developed
title: Grade 6 Central Idea Development Lesson
lessonExplanation: In Grade 6, central idea work moves beyond naming the topic. Students track how an idea is introduced, refined, and supported across multiple paragraphs.
workedExample: If an article begins with a problem, adds data from a study, and ends with a community solution, the central idea is developed through problem, evidence, and response.

choices: By introducing a problem, giving evidence, and explaining a response
By listing unrelated facts about the topic
By repeating the title in each paragraph
By ending with a personal opinion only
guidedPractice.passage: A school compost bin can turn fruit peels and leaves into rich soil. Students add plant scraps, mix the pile, and keep it slightly damp. Over time, tiny living things break down the scraps. The soil can help a garden grow.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which answer best explains how the author develops the central idea?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: Grade 6 students should explain the movement of the idea across the text, not just identify the topic.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: By introducing a problem, giving evidence, and explaining a response

standardLabel: Analyze central idea development and summary
title: Grade 6 Main Idea Lesson
lessonExplanation: Main idea is what the paragraph, section, or whole passage is mostly about. Grade 6 target: Analyze how a central idea develops across a full passage. Text complexity: Grade-level informational text with sections, examples, and shifts in focus. Evidence demand: Trace details across the beginning, middle, and end. Reasoning: Explain how details build, refine, or clarify the central idea.
workedExample: A Grade 6 central idea answer explains development: the text starts with a dry garden problem, adds test results, and ends with a combined solution.

choices: A combined water-saving plan solved more than one part of the schoolyard problem.
Students chose the first idea they tried.
The report is mostly about spring weather.
The tools worked exactly the same way.
guidedPractice.passage: During a dry spring, students redesigned part of the schoolyard to conserve water. Early observations showed that the garden needed more water than students could easily carry. Teams tested mulch, rain barrels, and drip irrigation, then compared weekly results. The strongest plan combined rain collection with slower watering because each tool solved a different part of the problem.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Analyze how a central idea develops across a full passage.
guidedPractice.coachHint: Trace details across the beginning, middle, and end.
guidedPractice.explanation: The best answer is "A combined water-saving plan solved more than one part of the schoolyard problem." because it meets this Grade 6 expectation: Analyze how a central idea develops through specific evidence.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: A combined water-saving plan solved more than one part of the schoolyard problem.

standardLabel: Summarize without opinion using key details
title: Grade 6 Objective Summary Lesson
lessonExplanation: An objective summary includes the central idea and the most important supporting details without personal reactions or minor examples.
workedExample: A strong summary of an article about school gardens would explain the main purpose, the key evidence, and the result, while leaving out whether the reader likes gardens.

choices: The article explains how student volunteers reduced waste by collecting lunch scraps for compost.
The article is boring because compost is not exciting.
The best part is the photograph of the garden.
Everyone should start composting immediately.
guidedPractice.passage: A school compost bin can turn fruit peels and leaves into rich soil. Students add plant scraps, mix the pile, and keep it slightly damp. Over time, tiny living things break down the scraps. The soil can help a garden grow.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which sentence belongs in an objective summary?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: This sentence reports an important idea from the text without opinion.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: The article explains how student volunteers reduced waste by collecting lunch scraps for compost.

standardLabel: Make inferences supported by textual evidence
title: Grade 6 Inference Lesson
lessonExplanation: Inference means using text clues plus what makes sense to figure out an idea the author does not state directly. Grade 6 target: Infer implied meaning and support it with textual evidence. Text complexity: Grade-level passage with subtle implications. Evidence demand: Cite evidence and explain the connection. Reasoning: Explain what the text implies but does not state.
workedExample: If each tool solved a different part of the problem, you can infer students understood the issue had more than one cause.

choices: The students understood that different tools could solve different parts of a problem.
The students avoided comparing results.
The project became less realistic over time.
The dry spring was not important.
guidedPractice.passage: During a dry spring, students redesigned part of the schoolyard to conserve water. Early observations showed that the garden needed more water than students could easily carry. Teams tested mulch, rain barrels, and drip irrigation, then compared weekly results. The strongest plan combined rain collection with slower watering because each tool solved a different part of the problem.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Infer implied meaning and support it with textual evidence.
guidedPractice.coachHint: Cite evidence and explain the connection.
guidedPractice.explanation: The best answer is "The students understood that different tools could solve different parts of a problem." because it meets this Grade 6 expectation: Make a defensible inference with evidence and explanation.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: The students understood that different tools could solve different parts of a problem.

standardLabel: Cite textual evidence to support analysis
title: Grade 6 Text Evidence Lesson
lessonExplanation: Text evidence is the detail, sentence, event, or quotation from the passage that proves an answer. Grade 6 target: Cite textual evidence to support analysis and inference. Text complexity: Grade-level passage with explicit and implied ideas. Evidence demand: Use precise evidence tied to a claim. Reasoning: Connect evidence to analysis.
workedExample: For the claim that the strongest plan solved multiple problems, cite the sentence explaining that rain collection and slower watering each solved a different part.

choices: The strongest plan combined rain collection with slower watering because each tool solved a different part of the problem.
Students worked during spring.
Teams compared weekly results.
The schoolyard had a garden.
guidedPractice.passage: During a dry spring, students redesigned part of the schoolyard to conserve water. Early observations showed that the garden needed more water than students could easily carry. Teams tested mulch, rain barrels, and drip irrigation, then compared weekly results. The strongest plan combined rain collection with slower watering because each tool solved a different part of the problem.

standardLabel: Determine author's purpose or point of view
skill: Author's Purpose and Point of View
title: Grade 6 Author's Purpose and Point of View Lesson
lessonExplanation: Author's purpose is why the author writes. Point of view is the author's position or attitude. Use word choice, selected details, and omitted details as clues.
workedExample: An author who calls a plan practical and carefully tested likely wants readers to view the plan as reasonable.

choices: The author describes the proposal as a careful solution supported by data.
The article has six paragraphs.
The title uses four words.
The passage includes a comma in sentence two.
guidedPractice.passage: A school compost bin can turn fruit peels and leaves into rich soil. Students add plant scraps, mix the pile, and keep it slightly damp. Over time, tiny living things break down the scraps. The soil can help a garden grow.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which clue best reveals the author's point of view?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: Evaluative words and selected evidence reveal the author's attitude.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: The author describes the proposal as a careful solution supported by data.

standardLabel: Analyze how structure organizes ideas
title: Grade 6 Text Structure and Author Organization Lesson
lessonExplanation: Grade 6 readers analyze how structure helps the author explain ideas. Look for comparison, cause and effect, problem and solution, chronology, and description.
workedExample: If a passage explains a shortage, lists causes, and describes possible fixes, the structure helps readers understand why the problem happened and what could be done.

choices: To show why an event happened and what resulted from it
To hide the main idea from readers
To make every paragraph sound the same
To avoid using evidence
guidedPractice.passage: A school compost bin can turn fruit peels and leaves into rich soil. Students add plant scraps, mix the pile, and keep it slightly damp. Over time, tiny living things break down the scraps. The soil can help a garden grow.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Why would an author use a cause-and-effect structure?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: Cause-and-effect structure connects reasons with outcomes.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: To show why an event happened and what resulted from it

standardLabel: Determine technical and academic word meanings
title: Grade 6 Technical Vocabulary in Context Lesson
lessonExplanation: Technical words belong to a subject area. Use context, examples, and nearby explanations to determine precise meanings.
workedExample: In an article about water systems, filtration means removing particles from water; the surrounding details about screens and layers help confirm the meaning.

standardLabel: Analyze connotation and tone in word choice
title: Grade 6 Tone and Connotation Lesson
lessonExplanation: Connotation is the feeling a word suggests. Tone is the author's attitude. In Grade 6, connect word choice to how the reader understands the topic.
workedExample: Calling a solution bold suggests admiration, while calling it risky suggests caution or concern.

choices: The committee proposed an untested plan.

guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which word choice creates a cautious tone?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: Untested suggests concern about whether the plan will work.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: The committee proposed an untested plan.

standardLabel: Trace and evaluate claims, reasons, and evidence
title: Grade 6 Claims, Reasons, and Evidence Lesson
lessonExplanation: Arguments include a claim, reasons, and evidence. At Grade 6, students trace whether the evidence actually supports the reason and whether the reason supports the claim.
workedExample: Claim: Schools should add refill stations. Reason: They reduce plastic waste. Evidence: A nearby school used 2,000 fewer disposable bottles after installing stations.

choices: Students recorded plant growth each week and used the data in science notebooks.
The garden gate is painted blue.
Some students like carrots more than peas.
The garden is behind the cafeteria.
guidedPractice.passage: A school compost bin can turn fruit peels and leaves into rich soil. Students add plant scraps, mix the pile, and keep it slightly damp. Over time, tiny living things break down the scraps. The soil can help a garden grow.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which evidence best supports the claim that school gardens can improve science learning?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: The evidence directly connects the garden to science learning.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Students recorded plant growth each week and used the data in science notebooks.

standardLabel: Compare how texts present similar information
title: Grade 6 Compare Text Presentations Lesson
lessonExplanation: When two texts cover a similar topic, compare what each one emphasizes, what evidence each includes, and how their purposes differ.
workedExample: A timeline may emphasize sequence, while an article may explain causes and effects. Both can teach the same topic in different ways.

choices: One text explains the inventor's problem, while the other shows a timeline of testing and revisions.
Both texts are printed on a page.
One text has a longer title.
Both texts include the word invention.
guidedPractice.passage: A school compost bin can turn fruit peels and leaves into rich soil. Students add plant scraps, mix the pile, and keep it slightly damp. Over time, tiny living things break down the scraps. The soil can help a garden grow.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which comparison best analyzes two texts about the same invention?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: This comparison explains how the texts present information differently.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: One text explains the inventor's problem, while the other shows a timeline of testing and revisions.

choices: One text explains the inventor's problem, while the other shows a timeline of testing and revisions.

standardLabel: Determine theme or central idea and summarize
title: Grade 6 Theme Lesson
lessonExplanation: Theme is the message or lesson a story suggests about life. A theme is usually a complete idea, not one word. Grade 6 target: Analyze how theme develops through plot and character. Text complexity: Literary text with conflict, change, and tension. Evidence demand: Use evidence from conflict and resolution. Reasoning: Explain how events build the theme over time.
workedExample: A stronger upper-grade theme explains a larger idea: responsible problem solving requires evidence, revision, and cooperation.

choices: Responsible problem solving grows from evidence, revision, and cooperation.
Fast answers are always best.
Community problems cannot be solved.
Data makes decisions weaker.
guidedPractice.passage: During a dry spring, students redesigned part of the schoolyard to conserve water. Early observations showed that the garden needed more water than students could easily carry. Teams tested mulch, rain barrels, and drip irrigation, then compared weekly results. The strongest plan combined rain collection with slower watering because each tool solved a different part of the problem.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Analyze how theme develops through plot and character.
guidedPractice.coachHint: Use evidence from conflict and resolution.
guidedPractice.explanation: The best answer is "Responsible problem solving grows from evidence, revision, and cooperation." because it meets this Grade 6 expectation: Analyze theme development across the text.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Responsible problem solving grows from evidence, revision, and cooperation.

standardLabel: Analyze theme development with evidence
title: Grade 6 Theme Development Lesson
lessonExplanation: At Grade 6, theme is not a one-word topic. Students identify a message and explain how it develops through conflict, character decisions, and the ending.
workedExample: A story about honesty may develop the theme that telling the truth can be difficult but repairs trust.

choices: Courage grows when people act even though they are afraid.
Courage
A student climbs a hill.
The story happens in autumn.
guidedPractice.passage: Nora wanted to build the tallest block tower in the room. When it fell twice, she took a breath, sorted the blocks by size, and tried again. Her friend Eli held the base steady. This time, the tower stood until cleanup.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which statement is a theme rather than a topic?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: A theme states a message about a topic.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Courage grows when people act even though they are afraid.

masteryCheck.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
masteryCheck.explanation: A theme states a message about a topic. This item adds another chance to apply the same skill with the Grade 6 evidence expectation.
masteryCheck.correctAnswer: Courage grows when people act even though they are afraid.

standardLabel: Analyze character response and conflict
title: Grade 6 Character Change and Conflict Lesson
lessonExplanation: Analyze how characters respond to conflict and how those responses reveal traits, motivations, or growth.
workedExample: A character who begins by avoiding responsibility but later admits a mistake has changed because of the conflict.

choices: At first Lena blames others, but later she apologizes and repairs the project.
Lena owns a blue notebook.
Lena's classroom has a clock.
Lena walks down the hallway.
guidedPractice.passage: Nora wanted to build the tallest block tower in the room. When it fell twice, she took a breath, sorted the blocks by size, and tried again. Her friend Eli held the base steady. This time, the tower stood until cleanup.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which evidence best supports that a character has changed?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: The contrast between earlier and later actions shows change.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: At first Lena blames others, but later she apologizes and repairs the project.

standardLabel: Analyze how plot unfolds through episodes
title: Grade 6 Plot Development in Episodes Lesson
lessonExplanation: Grade 6 readers analyze how episodes or scenes build conflict, reveal character, and move the plot toward a turning point or resolution.
workedExample: A scene where a character refuses help may increase conflict and set up a later moment when the character must change.

choices: The captain hides the map, causing the crew to question whom they can trust.
The ship has three sails.
The sky is gray.
The chapter title is short.
guidedPractice.passage: Nora wanted to build the tallest block tower in the room. When it fell twice, she took a breath, sorted the blocks by size, and tried again. Her friend Eli held the base steady. This time, the tower stood until cleanup.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which detail best shows an episode that develops the plot?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: This action creates conflict and changes what happens next.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: The captain hides the map, causing the crew to question whom they can trust.

standardLabel: Explain how point of view is developed
title: Grade 6 Point of View Lesson
lessonExplanation: Point of view is who is telling the story or how an author sees a topic. Grade 6 target: Explain how an author develops point of view. Text complexity: Text with clear narrator or author perspective. Evidence demand: Use word choice and selected details. Reasoning: Explain how details reveal viewpoint.
workedExample: The author emphasizes evidence, revision, and realistic maintenance, revealing a point of view that values practical problem solving.

choices: The author presents evidence-based problem solving as valuable and realistic.
The author treats the project as a joke.
The author avoids explaining the recommendation.
The author believes routines do not matter.
guidedPractice.passage: During a dry spring, students redesigned part of the schoolyard to conserve water. Early observations showed that the garden needed more water than students could easily carry. Teams tested mulch, rain barrels, and drip irrigation, then compared weekly results. The strongest plan combined rain collection with slower watering because each tool solved a different part of the problem.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Explain how an author develops point of view.
guidedPractice.coachHint: Use word choice and selected details.
guidedPractice.explanation: The best answer is "The author presents evidence-based problem solving as valuable and realistic." because it meets this Grade 6 expectation: Analyze development of point of view.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: The author presents evidence-based problem solving as valuable and realistic.

choices: The author presents evidence-based problem solving as valuable and realistic.

standardLabel: Analyze how structure contributes to meaning
title: Grade 6 Story Structure and Flashback Lesson
lessonExplanation: Authors may use flashback, pacing, or scene order to reveal information. Ask how the structure affects your understanding of character, conflict, or theme.
workedExample: A flashback to a broken promise can explain why a character is afraid to trust someone in the present.

choices: To reveal earlier events that explain a character's current choice
To remove all conflict from the story
To define every vocabulary word
To change the genre from fiction to nonfiction
guidedPractice.passage: Nora wanted to build the tallest block tower in the room. When it fell twice, she took a breath, sorted the blocks by size, and tried again. Her friend Eli held the base steady. This time, the tower stood until cleanup.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Why might an author include a flashback?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: Flashbacks often add background that deepens the present conflict.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: To reveal earlier events that explain a character's current choice

standardLabel: Analyze figurative, connotative, and technical meanings
title: Grade 6 Connotation and Figurative Language Lesson
lessonExplanation: Connotation and figurative language shape meaning. Look for words or phrases that suggest feelings, images, or nonliteral ideas, then explain their effect. Grade 6 target: Determine figurative and connotative meanings. Text complexity: Grade-level passage with tone and imagery. Evidence demand: Use context and phrase-level evidence. Reasoning: Explain effect on tone, mood, or meaning.
workedExample: If an author calls a hallway "a river of students," the phrase does not mean real water. It suggests many students moving quickly together. Grade 6 expectation: Analyze how word choice affects meaning.

choices: It uses a comparison to shape tone and help readers picture the movement.
It is a literal statement about water.
It weakens the image.
It proves the author dislikes students.
guidedPractice.passage: During a dry spring, students redesigned part of the schoolyard to conserve water. Early observations showed that the garden needed more water than students could easily carry. Teams tested mulch, rain barrels, and drip irrigation, then compared weekly results. The strongest plan combined rain collection with slower watering because each tool solved a different part of the problem.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Determine figurative and connotative meanings.
guidedPractice.coachHint: Use context and phrase-level evidence.
guidedPractice.explanation: The best answer is "It uses a comparison to shape tone and help readers picture the movement." because it meets this Grade 6 expectation: Analyze how word choice affects meaning.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: It uses a comparison to shape tone and help readers picture the movement.

standardLabel: Write informative texts with organized ideas
title: Grade 6 Informative Essay Organization Lesson
lessonExplanation: Informative writing explains a topic with an introduction, grouped ideas, facts, examples, transitions, and a conclusion.
workedExample: A report about wetlands could group details by habitat, flood protection, and threats, rather than listing facts randomly.

choices: Define renewable energy, explain two types, compare benefits and challenges, and conclude with why it matters.
List random facts about wind, sunlight, and batteries.
Begin with an opinion and avoid examples.
Describe only the color of solar panels.
guidedPractice.passage: A good response stays focused on the prompt. It gives a clear idea, includes details from the text, and explains why those details matter.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which plan best organizes an informative essay about renewable energy?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: The plan groups related information in a logical order.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Define renewable energy, explain two types, compare benefits and challenges, and conclude with why it matters.

standardLabel: Use punctuation to set off elements
title: Grade 6 Commas, Parentheses, and Dashes Lesson
lessonExplanation: Use punctuation to set off extra or interrupting information. The punctuation should make the sentence easier to read without changing the main idea.
workedExample: The model, which used recycled cardboard, held the most weight.

choices: The rain barrel, an inexpensive tool, collected runoff from the roof.
The rain barrel an inexpensive tool collected, runoff from the roof.
The rain, barrel an inexpensive tool collected runoff from the roof.
The rain barrel an inexpensive tool collected runoff, from the roof.

standardLabel: Conventions of Standard English - Formal and Informal Style
title: Grade 6 Formal and Informal Style Lesson
lessonExplanation: Conventions questions ask you to make writing clear and correct. Read the whole paragraph first, then check pronouns, verb tense, punctuation, and style in context.
workedExample: If a sentence says, "A writer should reread the draft because you may notice missing evidence," the pronoun shifts from "a writer" to "you." A better revision keeps the same person: "A writer should reread the draft because the writer may notice missing evidence."

choices: Sentence 2 should be revised to maintain formal style.
Sentence 1 should remove the word students.
Sentence 3 should change class to classes.
Sentence 4 should use informal language.
guidedPractice.passage: (1) When students revise an essay, they should check whether each detail supports the claim. (2) A writer should reread the draft because you may notice missing evidence. (3) The class discussed its ideas before writing the final paragraph. (4) Clear organization helps readers follow the explanation.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Read the paragraph. Which sentence should be revised?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Check whether the sentence keeps the same person and style from beginning to end.
guidedPractice.explanation: Sentence 2 shifts from a general noun to you. Keeping pronouns consistent makes the writing clearer and more formal.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Sentence 2 should be revised to maintain formal style.

standardLabel: Demonstrate command of pronoun conventions
title: Grade 6 Pronoun Case and Agreement Lesson
lessonExplanation: Pronouns must fit their role in a sentence and agree with the noun they replace. Check whether the pronoun is used as a subject, object, or possessive.
workedExample: In 'Maya and I presented the model,' I is correct because it is part of the subject.

choices: Maya and I explained the design.
Him and Maya explained the design.
The teacher gave Maya and I notes.
Us finished the model.
guidedPractice.passage: The students revised a paragraph about a class project. They checked each sentence for clear meaning, correct punctuation, and words that matched the formal style of the report.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which underlined pronoun is used correctly?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: I is correct as part of the subject of the sentence.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Maya and I explained the design.

standardLabel: Maintain pronoun consistency in writing
skill: Shifts in Pronoun Person and Number
title: Grade 6 Shifts in Pronoun Person and Number Lesson
lessonExplanation: Avoid confusing shifts in pronoun person or number. Keep the point of view consistent unless there is a clear reason to change.
workedExample: A writer should revise, 'When students revise, you should check evidence' because it shifts from students to you.

choices: When students revise an essay, you should check every claim.
Students should revise an essay before submitting it.
Writers can improve clarity by rereading drafts.
A student should check whether each detail supports the claim.
guidedPractice.passage: The students revised a paragraph about a class project. They checked each sentence for clear meaning, correct punctuation, and words that matched the formal style of the report.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which sentence contains an inappropriate shift in pronoun person?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: The sentence shifts from students to you.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: When students revise an essay, you should check every claim.

standardLabel: Use sentence variety for meaning and style
title: Grade 6 Varying Sentence Patterns Lesson
lessonExplanation: Sentence variety helps writing sound clear and natural. Combine short related ideas and vary beginnings while keeping meaning precise.
workedExample: Instead of 'The team tested the bridge. The team recorded data,' write 'After testing the bridge, the team recorded data.'

choices: After the committee reviewed the data, it changed the schedule.
The committee reviewed data. The committee changed schedule.
Reviewed the committee data schedule changed.
The data was schedule committee reviewed.
guidedPractice.passage: The students revised a paragraph about a class project. They checked each sentence for clear meaning, correct punctuation, and words that matched the formal style of the report.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which revision improves sentence variety without changing the meaning?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: The revision combines ideas clearly and varies the sentence opening.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: After the committee reviewed the data, it changed the schedule.

choices: After the committee reviewed the data, it changed the schedule.
The committee reviewed data. The committee changed schedule.

The data was schedule committee reviewed.

standardLabel: Write arguments with clear reasons and evidence
title: Grade 6 Argument Essay Evidence Lesson
lessonExplanation: Argument essay evidence is proof that helps a reader believe your claim. In Grade 6, strong evidence does three jobs: it connects directly to the claim, it gives a specific fact or detail from the text, and it can be explained in your own words. Weak evidence may be true, but it is too general, off-topic, or impossible to connect to the reason you are trying to prove.
workedExample: Claim: The library should stay open after school. Strong evidence: the survey showed that many sixth graders do not have a quiet place to work right after school. Why it works: the evidence directly supports the reason that students need a supervised place to read, research, and finish assignments.

choices: A recent student survey showed that 64 percent of sixth graders do not have a quiet place to work immediately after school.
The school library has research books and laptops.
Some students play sports after school.
The student council is debating the library schedule.
guidedPractice.passage: The student council is debating whether the school library should stay open two afternoons each week. A recent student survey showed that 64 percent of sixth graders do not have a quiet place to work immediately after school. The librarian also reported that research books and laptops are checked out most often during the final fifteen minutes before the building closes. Some students said they would use the extra time to finish group projects before sports or sibling-care responsibilities begin.
guidedPractice.question: Which sentence gives the strongest evidence for the claim that students need an after-school study space?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Ask, 'Does this detail prove the reason, or is it just related to the topic?'
guidedPractice.explanation: The survey detail is specific and directly supports the claim that students need a quiet study space.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: A recent student survey showed that 64 percent of sixth graders do not have a quiet place to work immediately after school.

choices: relevant
decorative
repeated
surprising
guidedPractice.passage: A strong argument uses evidence that is relevant to the claim and specific enough to explain.
guidedPractice.question: Choose the word that tells what evidence must be to support an argument.
guidedPractice.coachHint: The best word describes evidence that actually fits the claim.
guidedPractice.explanation: Relevant evidence connects to the claim instead of drifting to another topic.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: relevant

choices: Research books and laptops are checked out most often right before the building closes.
The library is part of the school building.
The debate is happening in student council.
Some students walk past the library each day.
guidedPractice.passage: The student council is debating whether the school library should stay open two afternoons each week. A recent student survey showed that 64 percent of sixth graders do not have a quiet place to work immediately after school. The librarian also reported that research books and laptops are checked out most often during the final fifteen minutes before the building closes. Some students said they would use the extra time to finish group projects before sports or sibling-care responsibilities begin.
guidedPractice.question: Which detail is strong support for the reason that more library time would help students complete schoolwork?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Strong support usually includes a concrete fact, number, action, or result.
guidedPractice.explanation: Checkout demand right before closing proves students are trying to use library resources and may need more time.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Research books and laptops are checked out most often right before the building closes.

choices: The garden club already has donated seeds and science classes can collect growth data.
The playground is used during recess.
Native plants can be different colors.
Some students have seen gardens before.
guidedPractice.passage: A class committee wrote an argument about adding a small native-plant garden near the playground. Their notes show that the garden club already has donated seeds, two science classes can collect growth data, and the custodian identified a safe corner away from bus traffic. One concern is that the garden would need watering during dry weeks, so the committee suggested assigning rotating teams.
guidedPractice.question: Which evidence best supports the claim that the native-plant garden is realistic for the school to start?
guidedPractice.coachHint: For an argument about whether a plan can work, choose evidence about resources, safety, time, or results.
guidedPractice.explanation: This evidence shows the school has resources and an academic purpose, so it supports feasibility.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: The garden club already has donated seeds and science classes can collect growth data.

choices: safe corner away from bus traffic
donated seeds
dry weeks
playground
guidedPractice.passage: A class committee wrote an argument about adding a small native-plant garden near the playground. Their notes show that the garden club already has donated seeds, two science classes can collect growth data, and the custodian identified a safe corner away from bus traffic. One concern is that the garden would need watering during dry weeks, so the committee suggested assigning rotating teams.
guidedPractice.question: Explain why the safe corner away from bus traffic is useful evidence for the garden argument.
guidedPractice.coachHint: Use because: 'This supports the claim because...'
guidedPractice.explanation: A useful explanation should connect the safe location to the claim that the plan is practical and responsible.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: safe corner away from bus traffic

choices: A pilot cart served 118 students in three mornings without delaying first period.
The main entrance is near the hallway.
Some students skip breakfast.
The cafeteria line can be long.
guidedPractice.passage: Sixth graders are writing to the principal about adding a grab-and-go breakfast cart near the main entrance. Attendance records show that students who arrive just before homeroom often skip breakfast because the cafeteria line is long. The nurse reported that several students visit before lunch with headaches or stomachaches. A pilot cart used during testing week served 118 students in three mornings without delaying first period.
guidedPractice.question: Which evidence best supports the claim that a breakfast cart could help students without making them late?
guidedPractice.coachHint: The strongest evidence often answers the concern someone might raise against the claim.
guidedPractice.explanation: This detail includes a result from a trial and addresses both student need and the concern about being late.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: A pilot cart served 118 students in three mornings without delaying first period.

choices: Students who arrive just before homeroom often skip breakfast because the cafeteria line is long.
The nurse has an office near the cafeteria.
Breakfast carts can have wheels.
The main entrance opens in the morning.

Attendance records show that students who arrive just before homeroom often skip breakfast because the cafeteria line is long. The nurse reported that several students visit before lunch with headaches or stomachaches. A pilot cart used during testing week served 118 students in three mornings without delaying first period.
independentPractice.question: Which detail strongly supports the reason that some students need faster breakfast access?
independentPractice.coachHint: Match evidence to the exact reason, not just the general topic.
independentPractice.explanation: This evidence explains the problem the proposed cart is meant to solve.
independentPractice.correctAnswer: Students who arrive just before homeroom often skip breakfast because the cafeteria line is long.

choices: explains how
copies without
changes why
ignores how
independentPractice.passage: Evidence is strongest when the writer explains how the detail supports the claim.
independentPractice.question: Choose the phrase that shows what a writer must do after giving evidence.
independentPractice.coachHint: After evidence, ask, 'So what does this prove?'
independentPractice.explanation: Argument writing should not drop in evidence and move on. The writer must explain the connection.
independentPractice.correctAnswer: explains how

choices: The nurse reported that several students visit before lunch with headaches or stomachaches.

The cafeteria line is long.

independentPractice.passage: Sixth graders are writing to the principal about adding a grab-and-go breakfast cart near the main entrance. Attendance records show that students who arrive just before homeroom often skip breakfast because the cafeteria line is long. The nurse reported that several students visit before lunch with headaches or stomachaches. A pilot cart used during testing week served 118 students in three mornings without delaying first period.
independentPractice.question: Which sentence best proves that the problem affects student well-being, not just convenience?
independentPractice.coachHint: Look for evidence that matches the exact phrase in the question.
independentPractice.explanation: The nurse's report shows a possible health effect connected to skipped breakfast.
independentPractice.correctAnswer: The nurse reported that several students visit before lunch with headaches or stomachaches.

choices: The library walls were painted blue last summer.

Research books and laptops are checked out near closing time.

A recent student survey showed that 64 percent of sixth graders do not have a quiet place to work immediately after school. The librarian also reported that research books and laptops are checked out most often during the final fifteen minutes before the building closes. Some students said they would use the extra time to finish group projects before sports or sibling-care responsibilities begin.
independentPractice.question: Which evidence would be weak or off-topic for an essay arguing that library hours should be extended?
independentPractice.coachHint: A weak detail may be true but does not help prove the claim.
independentPractice.explanation: The wall color is off-topic; the other details can support the argument.
independentPractice.correctAnswer: A survey showed many students lack a quiet place after school.

independentPractice.coachHint: Use this frame: 'The detail ___ supports the claim because ___.'
independentPractice.explanation: A strong response names one text detail and explains how it proves the claim.
independentPractice.correctAnswer: quiet place

choices: Custodians counted 47 empty plastic bottles in gym trash cans during one week.
The gym floor is shiny.

exitTicket.passage: A class wants to argue that the school should add recycling bins near the gym. Custodians counted 47 empty plastic bottles in gym trash cans during one week, and the environmental club offered to make reminder signs.
exitTicket.question: Which evidence best supports the recycling-bin claim?
exitTicket.coachHint: Specific data is often stronger than a vague observation.
exitTicket.explanation: The counted bottles prove there is recyclable material being thrown away near the gym.
exitTicket.correctAnswer: Custodians counted 47 empty plastic bottles in gym trash cans during one week.

choices: relevant
random

exitTicket.question: Choose the word that means the evidence fits the claim.
exitTicket.coachHint: Think: does this proof belong with this claim?

choices: Six teachers are willing to volunteer, and 38 students requested extra help.
The room would need chairs.
Homework is sometimes difficult.

masteryCheck.passage: Students are arguing that the school should create a homework help room twice a week. A teacher survey showed that six teachers are willing to volunteer once a month, and last quarter 38 students requested extra help after missing assignments.
masteryCheck.question: Which evidence best supports the claim that a homework help room is needed and possible?
masteryCheck.coachHint: The best evidence can sometimes support more than one part of the claim.
masteryCheck.explanation: This evidence proves both student need and available support.
masteryCheck.correctAnswer: Six teachers are willing to volunteer, and 38 students requested extra help.

Last year, more than 120 families attended the event, but most activities ended after fifteen minutes. The club's sponsor wrote that the play would last twelve minutes and would use props already stored in the auditorium. Students also said the performance would give families a reason to stay for the full evening.
masteryCheck.question: Which sentence best supports the claim that the drama club's plan is practical?
masteryCheck.coachHint: Practical evidence often shows a plan can work with available time or resources.
masteryCheck.explanation: The sentence addresses time and materials, which are practical concerns.
masteryCheck.correctAnswer: The club's sponsor wrote that the play would last twelve minutes and would use props already stored in the auditorium.

masteryCheck.question: Which detail is strong support for the shared-headphones claim?
masteryCheck.coachHint: Look for evidence that proves the problem is real.
masteryCheck.explanation: The number shows the problem happens often enough to justify a solution.
masteryCheck.correctAnswer: Students forgot headphones 92 times during the first quarter.

masteryCheck.coachHint: Connect the evidence to the reason the change matters.
masteryCheck.explanation: The detail shows the problem affects instruction, so shared headphones could solve more than a small inconvenience.
masteryCheck.correctAnswer: lost class time

choices: prove the claim
decorate the paragraph
change the topic

masteryCheck.passage: The strongest argument evidence is specific enough to prove the claim.
masteryCheck.question: Choose the phrase that explains why strong evidence works.
masteryCheck.coachHint: Evidence is proof, not decoration.
masteryCheck.explanation: Evidence belongs in an argument because it proves the claim and gives the writer something to explain.
masteryCheck.correctAnswer: prove the claim

retestRecommendation: After this lesson, assign a short argument paragraph where students underline one claim, one reason, and the evidence that best proves it.

standardLabel: Use evidence and analysis in text-dependent writing
title: Grade 6 TDA Evidence and Explanation Lesson
lessonExplanation: A strong TDA does not retell the passage. It answers the prompt with a clear claim, uses evidence from the text, and explains how the evidence proves the claim.
workedExample: Claim: The character learns to be more responsible. Evidence: The character returns to fix the mistake without being asked. Explanation: This detail matters because the character chooses responsibility even when no one is forcing the choice.

choices: Add a sentence that explains how the evidence proves the claim.
Copy a longer sentence from the passage.
Start a new paragraph with no evidence.
Replace the claim with a summary of the story.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which revision best teaches a Grade 6 writer to explain evidence instead of just listing it?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Use because to connect the evidence back to the claim.
guidedPractice.explanation: A TDA needs evidence and analysis. The explanation shows why the evidence matters.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Add a sentence that explains how the evidence proves the claim.

standardLabel: Conduct short research and integrate sources
title: Grade 6 Research Source Integration Lesson
lessonExplanation: When using research, combine information from sources in your own words and make clear how the evidence supports the point.
workedExample: A student might combine a chart about rainfall with an article about drought-resistant plants to explain why one garden design is practical.

choices: The chart shows rainfall is decreasing, and the article explains that native plants need less watering, so native plants would fit the school's dry garden site.
Both sources have titles.
The chart is on the first page and the article is on the second.
I liked the article better than the chart.
guidedPractice.passage: A good response stays focused on the prompt. It gives a clear idea, includes details from the text, and explains why those details matter.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which sentence best integrates information from two sources?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: This sentence combines source information and explains why it matters.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: The chart shows rainfall is decreasing, and the article explains that native plants need less watering, so native plants would fit the school's dry garden site.

standardLabel: Analyze central idea development across sections
title: Grade 7 Central Idea Across Sections Lesson
lessonExplanation: In Grade 7, students analyze how a central idea grows across sections of a text. Track how the author introduces the idea, complicates it, and uses evidence to refine it.
workedExample: An article may begin by defining a problem, then use statistics, expert explanation, and an example to show why the problem is more complex than it first appears.

choices: The author introduces a concern, adds data that complicates it, and ends by explaining a possible response.
The author mentions the topic once and then changes subjects.
The author repeats the title without adding evidence.
The author gives only a personal opinion.
guidedPractice.passage: A school compost bin can turn fruit peels and leaves into rich soil. Students add plant scraps, mix the pile, and keep it slightly damp. Over time, tiny living things break down the scraps. The soil can help a garden grow.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which answer best analyzes how a central idea develops across the text?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: Grade 7 central idea analysis should explain how sections work together to develop the idea.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: The author introduces a concern, adds data that complicates it, and ends by explaining a possible response.

standardLabel: Analyze central idea development and objective summary
title: Grade 7 Main Idea Lesson
lessonExplanation: Main idea is what the paragraph, section, or whole passage is mostly about. Grade 7 target: Analyze how two or more central ideas develop and interact. Text complexity: Multi-section informational text with related central ideas. Evidence demand: Use evidence for more than one central idea. Reasoning: Explain relationships among central ideas, not just list them.
workedExample: A Grade 7 answer tracks interacting central ideas: reducing waste matters, and maintaining routines matters. The final recommendation connects both.

choices: Successful conservation depends on reducing waste and maintaining routines over time.
The report gives only one central idea.
Rain barrels are mentioned but never evaluated.
Student routines are unrelated to conservation.
guidedPractice.passage: A community science team evaluated how a schoolyard could conserve water while supporting plant growth. Their first central finding was that conservation required reducing waste; their second was that long-term maintenance mattered as much as the first week of results. The final recommendation connected both ideas by pairing rain barrels with simple student routines for checking overflow and soil moisture.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Analyze how two or more central ideas develop and interact.
guidedPractice.coachHint: Use evidence for more than one central idea.
guidedPractice.explanation: The best answer is "Successful conservation depends on reducing waste and maintaining routines over time." because it meets this Grade 7 expectation: Explain how central ideas interact and how details shape them.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Successful conservation depends on reducing waste and maintaining routines over time.

masteryCheck.explanation: The best answer is "Successful conservation depends on reducing waste and maintaining routines over time." because it meets this Grade 7 expectation: Explain how central ideas interact and how details shape them.
masteryCheck.correctAnswer: Successful conservation depends on reducing waste and maintaining routines over time.

choices: Successful conservation depends on reducing waste and maintaining routines over time.
The report gives only one central idea.
Rain barrels are mentioned but never evaluated.
Student routines are unrelated to conservation.
masteryCheck.passage: A community science team evaluated how a schoolyard could conserve water while supporting plant growth. Their first central finding was that conservation required reducing waste; their second was that long-term maintenance mattered as much as the first week of results. The final recommendation connected both ideas by pairing rain barrels with simple student routines for checking overflow and soil moisture.

standardLabel: Draw inferences supported by multiple text details
title: Grade 7 Inference Lesson
lessonExplanation: Inference means using text clues plus what makes sense to figure out an idea the author does not state directly. Grade 7 target: Infer motives, claims, bias, or deeper meaning. Text complexity: Text with layered character, author, or argument clues. Evidence demand: Use precise evidence from across the text. Reasoning: Analyze why evidence matters and what it reveals.
workedExample: If the team recommends routines for checking overflow and moisture, you can infer they care about whether the solution lasts.

choices: The team valued solutions students could maintain after the first test period.
The team cared only about the first week.
The team ignored soil moisture.
The report rejects student routines.
guidedPractice.passage: A community science team evaluated how a schoolyard could conserve water while supporting plant growth. Their first central finding was that conservation required reducing waste; their second was that long-term maintenance mattered as much as the first week of results. The final recommendation connected both ideas by pairing rain barrels with simple student routines for checking overflow and soil moisture.

standardLabel: Cite several pieces of textual evidence to support analysis
title: Grade 7 Text Evidence Lesson
lessonExplanation: Text evidence is the detail, sentence, event, or quotation from the passage that proves an answer. Grade 7 target: Use several pieces of evidence to support analysis. Text complexity: Complex text with multiple relevant details. Evidence demand: Use evidence from different parts of a text. Reasoning: Analyze patterns across evidence.
workedExample: For the claim that maintenance matters, cite the recommendation about checking overflow and soil moisture.

choices: The recommendation paired rain barrels with student routines for checking overflow and soil moisture.
The team evaluated a schoolyard.
The text mentions plant growth.
The project had a final recommendation.
guidedPractice.passage: A community science team evaluated how a schoolyard could conserve water while supporting plant growth. The final recommendation connected both ideas by pairing rain barrels with simple student routines for checking overflow and soil moisture.

standardLabel: Analyze author's purpose and point of view
title: Grade 7 Author Purpose and Rhetorical Choices Lesson
lessonExplanation: Authors make choices to inform, persuade, or explain. Analyze how word choice, examples, and evidence reveal purpose and point of view.
workedExample: If an author uses phrases such as wasteful habit and practical alternative, the wording suggests a critical view of the habit and support for change.

choices: The author includes statistics and urgent word choice to persuade readers that action is needed.
The passage has a short title.
The article uses black text.
The final sentence has eight words.
guidedPractice.passage: A school compost bin can turn fruit peels and leaves into rich soil. Students add plant scraps, mix the pile, and keep it slightly damp. Over time, tiny living things break down the scraps. The soil can help a garden grow.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which detail best reveals the author's purpose?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: Purpose is revealed by evidence choices and tone.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: The author includes statistics and urgent word choice to persuade readers that action is needed.

standardLabel: Analyze text features and visual information
title: Grade 7 Data and Visual Information Lesson
lessonExplanation: Charts, tables, maps, and diagrams can add evidence or clarify relationships. Grade 7 students connect visual information to the author's claims and ideas.
workedExample: A line graph showing rising temperatures can support a paragraph explaining changes over time.

choices: Compare the table data with the article's claim to see how the evidence supports it.
Ignore the table because it is not a paragraph.
Read only the table title and skip the data.
Assume the table repeats every sentence exactly.
guidedPractice.passage: A school compost bin can turn fruit peels and leaves into rich soil. Students add plant scraps, mix the pile, and keep it slightly damp. Over time, tiny living things break down the scraps. The soil can help a garden grow.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: How should a reader use a table that appears with an article?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: Visual information should be integrated with the written text.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Compare the table data with the article's claim to see how the evidence supports it.

standardLabel: Analyze how text structure develops ideas
title: Grade 7 Text Structure Effects Lesson
lessonExplanation: Text structure affects how readers understand information. Analyze how comparison, cause and effect, chronology, or problem and solution supports the author's purpose.
workedExample: A text that alternates between a problem and several attempted solutions helps readers evaluate which response is most effective.

choices: It shows what challenge exists and how different responses address it.
It makes every paragraph unrelated.
It removes the need for evidence.
It turns an informational text into a story.
guidedPractice.passage: A school compost bin can turn fruit peels and leaves into rich soil. Students add plant scraps, mix the pile, and keep it slightly damp. Over time, tiny living things break down the scraps. The soil can help a garden grow.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: How does a problem-and-solution structure help the reader?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: This structure connects the issue to possible responses.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: It shows what challenge exists and how different responses address it.

standardLabel: Determine academic and domain-specific meanings
title: Grade 7 Precise Academic Vocabulary Lesson
lessonExplanation: Academic and technical words often have precise meanings. Use context, examples, contrast clues, and word parts to determine the best meaning.
workedExample: In an article about debate, position means a viewpoint or claim, not where someone is standing.

choices: A factor that can change during an investigation
A sentence with an unknown word
A person who refuses to decide
A type of punctuation
guidedPractice.passage: The class found a smooth stone beside the stream. Later, they carefully wrote notes about the shape, color, and size of each object they collected.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: In a passage about scientific testing, what does variable most likely mean?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: The science context gives variable a precise technical meaning.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: A factor that can change during an investigation

independentPractice.explanation: The science context gives variable a precise technical meaning.
independentPractice.correctAnswer: A factor that can change during an investigation

standardLabel: Evaluate claims, reasons, and evidence
title: Grade 7 Argument Claim Strength Lesson
lessonExplanation: Grade 7 students evaluate whether a claim is supported by logical reasons and relevant evidence. A strong argument connects claim, reason, evidence, and explanation.
workedExample: A claim about later school start times is stronger when supported by research about sleep and student attention, not just a statement that students prefer it.

choices: A student survey shows many students need internet access after school, and checkout records show high demand before closing.
The library sign is near the front door.
Some books are arranged alphabetically.
Evening is a word with seven letters.
guidedPractice.passage: A school compost bin can turn fruit peels and leaves into rich soil. Students add plant scraps, mix the pile, and keep it slightly damp. Over time, tiny living things break down the scraps. The soil can help a garden grow.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which evidence most strengthens the claim that the library should offer evening hours?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: This evidence is relevant, specific, and connected to the claim.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: A student survey shows many students need internet access after school, and checkout records show high demand before closing.

standardLabel: Compare how authors present similar topics
skill: Compare Authors on the Same Topic
title: Grade 7 Compare Authors on the Same Topic Lesson
lessonExplanation: When authors write about the same topic, compare their purpose, evidence, emphasis, and tone. Strong comparisons explain meaningful differences in presentation.
workedExample: One author may emphasize economic costs while another emphasizes environmental benefits. That difference changes how readers understand the issue.

choices: One author emphasizes community benefits, while the other focuses on the cost and possible drawbacks.
Both texts have paragraphs.
One text is printed above the other.
Both authors use periods.
guidedPractice.passage: A school compost bin can turn fruit peels and leaves into rich soil. Students add plant scraps, mix the pile, and keep it slightly damp. Over time, tiny living things break down the scraps. The soil can help a garden grow.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which comparison best analyzes two authors' presentations?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: This answer compares emphasis and perspective, not surface features.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: One author emphasizes community benefits, while the other focuses on the cost and possible drawbacks.

standardLabel: Analyze theme development and provide summary
title: Grade 7 Theme Lesson
lessonExplanation: Theme is the message or lesson a story suggests about life. A theme is usually a complete idea, not one word. Grade 7 target: Analyze theme interaction with story elements. Text complexity: Literary text with interacting setting, plot, and character choices. Evidence demand: Use evidence from multiple story elements. Reasoning: Explain how story elements shape theme.
workedExample: A stronger upper-grade theme explains a larger idea: responsible problem solving requires evidence, revision, and cooperation.

choices: Responsible problem solving grows from evidence, revision, and cooperation.
Fast answers are always best.
Community problems cannot be solved.
Data makes decisions weaker.
guidedPractice.passage: A community science team evaluated how a schoolyard could conserve water while supporting plant growth. Their first central finding was that conservation required reducing waste; their second was that long-term maintenance mattered as much as the first week of results. The final recommendation connected both ideas by pairing rain barrels with simple student routines for checking overflow and soil moisture.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Analyze theme interaction with story elements.
guidedPractice.coachHint: Use evidence from multiple story elements.
guidedPractice.explanation: The best answer is "Responsible problem solving grows from evidence, revision, and cooperation." because it meets this Grade 7 expectation: Analyze how theme emerges and is shaped by the story.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Responsible problem solving grows from evidence, revision, and cooperation.

Their first central finding was that conservation required reducing waste; their second was that long-term maintenance mattered as much as the first week of results. The final recommendation connected both ideas by pairing rain barrels with simple student routines for checking overflow and soil moisture.

independentPractice.coachHint: Use evidence from multiple story elements.
independentPractice.explanation: The best answer is "Responsible problem solving grows from evidence, revision, and cooperation." because it meets this Grade 7 expectation: Analyze how theme emerges and is shaped by the story.

standardLabel: Analyze character interaction and theme development
title: Grade 7 Character Motivation and Theme Lesson
lessonExplanation: Characters' motives and choices often reveal theme. Explain how a character's reason for acting connects to the message of the story.
workedExample: A character who risks embarrassment to defend a classmate may develop a theme about courage and responsibility.

choices: Because Amira wants to repair the harm she caused, her choice supports the theme that accountability can rebuild trust.
Amira wears a green jacket.
The story is written in paragraphs.
Amira walks quickly at the beginning.
guidedPractice.passage: Nora wanted to build the tallest block tower in the room. When it fell twice, she took a breath, sorted the blocks by size, and tried again. Her friend Eli held the base steady. This time, the tower stood until cleanup.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which answer best connects motivation to theme?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: This answer connects motive, action, and theme.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Because Amira wants to repair the harm she caused, her choice supports the theme that accountability can rebuild trust.

standardLabel: Analyze interaction of story elements
title: Grade 7 Plot Lines and Conflict Development Lesson
lessonExplanation: Grade 7 readers analyze how plot lines interact. Track conflicts, turning points, and how one event changes later choices.
workedExample: A competition plot may interact with a friendship conflict when a character must choose between winning and helping a teammate.

choices: After hiding the mistake, Nolan must decide whether to protect his score or tell the team what happened.
Nolan's pencil is blue.
The contest takes place on Tuesday.
The room has twenty chairs.
guidedPractice.passage: Nora wanted to build the tallest block tower in the room. When it fell twice, she took a breath, sorted the blocks by size, and tried again. Her friend Eli held the base steady. This time, the tower stood until cleanup.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which detail best shows conflict development?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: This detail creates a meaningful choice that develops conflict.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: After hiding the mistake, Nolan must decide whether to protect his score or tell the team what happened.

standardLabel: Analyze narrator point of view and perspective
title: Grade 7 Narrator Reliability and Perspective Lesson
lessonExplanation: A narrator's perspective shapes what readers know. Consider whether the narrator is limited, biased, mistaken, or leaving out information.
workedExample: If a narrator insists everyone is unfair but the dialogue shows others trying to help, readers may question the narrator's reliability.

choices: The narrator claims no one helped, but the dialogue shows two classmates offering support.
The narrator uses first-person pronouns.
The narrator describes the weather.
The narrator mentions a notebook.
guidedPractice.passage: Nora wanted to build the tallest block tower in the room. When it fell twice, she took a breath, sorted the blocks by size, and tried again. Her friend Eli held the base steady. This time, the tower stood until cleanup.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which detail suggests the narrator may be unreliable?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: A contradiction between narration and evidence can signal unreliability.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: The narrator claims no one helped, but the dialogue shows two classmates offering support.

guidedPractice.explanation: A contradiction between narration and evidence can signal unreliability.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: The narrator claims no one helped, but the dialogue shows two classmates offering support.

standardLabel: Analyze how point of view shapes a text
title: Grade 7 Point of View Lesson
lessonExplanation: Point of view is who is telling the story or how an author sees a topic. Grade 7 target: Analyze viewpoint, bias, and reliability. Text complexity: Text with limited or biased perspective. Evidence demand: Use omitted and emphasized details. Reasoning: Evaluate how perspective shapes claims or events.
workedExample: The author emphasizes evidence, revision, and realistic maintenance, revealing a point of view that values practical problem solving.

choices: The author presents evidence-based problem solving as valuable and realistic.
The author treats the project as a joke.
The author avoids explaining the recommendation.
The author believes routines do not matter.
guidedPractice.passage: A community science team evaluated how a schoolyard could conserve water while supporting plant growth. Their first central finding was that conservation required reducing waste; their second was that long-term maintenance mattered as much as the first week of results. The final recommendation connected both ideas by pairing rain barrels with simple student routines for checking overflow and soil moisture.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Analyze viewpoint, bias, and reliability.
guidedPractice.coachHint: Use omitted and emphasized details.
guidedPractice.explanation: The best answer is "The author presents evidence-based problem solving as valuable and realistic." because it meets this Grade 7 expectation: Analyze viewpoint and possible bias.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: The author presents evidence-based problem solving as valuable and realistic.

standardLabel: Analyze how structure contributes to meaning
title: Grade 7 Poetry Structure and Meaning Lesson
lessonExplanation: Poets use line breaks, stanzas, repetition, and sound patterns to shape meaning. Analyze how the structure emphasizes ideas or creates an effect.
workedExample: A repeated line can show that a speaker keeps returning to the same worry or hope.

choices: It can emphasize an important feeling or idea the speaker returns to.
It always proves the poem has no theme.
It makes every word literal.
It changes a poem into an essay.
guidedPractice.passage: Nora wanted to build the tallest block tower in the room. When it fell twice, she took a breath, sorted the blocks by size, and tried again. Her friend Eli held the base steady. This time, the tower stood until cleanup.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: How can repetition affect a poem's meaning?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: Repetition often draws attention to central feelings or ideas.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: It can emphasize an important feeling or idea the speaker returns to.

masteryCheck.explanation: Repetition often draws attention to central feelings or ideas. This item adds another chance to apply the same skill with the Grade 7 evidence expectation.
masteryCheck.correctAnswer: It can emphasize an important feeling or idea the speaker returns to.

retestRecommendation: After this lesson, assign one short Grade 7 practice item for Poetry Structure and Meaning with a fresh original passage.

standardLabel: Analyze figurative, connotative, and technical meanings
title: Grade 7 Connotation and Figurative Language Lesson
lessonExplanation: Connotation and figurative language shape meaning. Look for words or phrases that suggest feelings, images, or nonliteral ideas, then explain their effect. Grade 7 target: Analyze tone, mood, and deeper meaning. Text complexity: Text with nuanced language and implied tone. Evidence demand: Use clusters of words and images. Reasoning: Explain how language shapes interpretation.
workedExample: If an author calls a hallway "a river of students," the phrase does not mean real water. It suggests many students moving quickly together. Grade 7 expectation: Analyze tone and meaning created by language.

choices: It uses a comparison to shape tone and help readers picture the movement.
It is a literal statement about water.
It weakens the image.
It proves the author dislikes students.
guidedPractice.passage: A community science team evaluated how a schoolyard could conserve water while supporting plant growth. Their first central finding was that conservation required reducing waste; their second was that long-term maintenance mattered as much as the first week of results. The final recommendation connected both ideas by pairing rain barrels with simple student routines for checking overflow and soil moisture.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Analyze tone, mood, and deeper meaning.
guidedPractice.coachHint: Use clusters of words and images.
guidedPractice.explanation: The best answer is "It uses a comparison to shape tone and help readers picture the movement." because it meets this Grade 7 expectation: Analyze tone and meaning created by language.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: It uses a comparison to shape tone and help readers picture the movement.

standardLabel: Analyze how word choice affects meaning and tone
title: Grade 7 Mood, Tone, and Word Choice Lesson
lessonExplanation: Mood is the feeling created for the reader. Tone is the author's or speaker's attitude. Analyze how specific words, images, and figurative language create both.
workedExample: Words like dim, hollow, and motionless can create an uneasy mood even before a character says anything.

choices: uneasy, flickered, silent
bright, cheerful, simple
smooth, balanced, useful
ordinary, clear, expected
guidedPractice.passage: Nora wanted to build the tallest block tower in the room. When it fell twice, she took a breath, sorted the blocks by size, and tried again. Her friend Eli held the base steady. This time, the tower stood until cleanup.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which words most strongly create a tense mood?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: These words suggest uncertainty and suspense.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: uneasy, flickered, silent

standardLabel: Write informative texts with clear organization
title: Grade 7 Informative Writing with Transitions Lesson
lessonExplanation: Informative writing uses transitions to connect categories, examples, causes, effects, and comparisons so readers can follow complex information.
workedExample: Transitions such as however, as a result, and for example show how ideas relate.

choices: however
for example
as a result
first
guidedPractice.passage: A good response stays focused on the prompt. It gives a clear idea, includes details from the text, and explains why those details matter.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which transition best shows contrast?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: However signals a contrast between ideas.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: however

standardLabel: Apply grade-level punctuation conventions
title: Grade 7 Comma Use with Coordinate Adjectives Lesson
lessonExplanation: Use commas between coordinate adjectives when both adjectives equally describe the noun and can be joined by and.
workedExample: A clear, detailed explanation uses a comma because clear and detailed both describe explanation.

choices: The students wrote a careful, thoughtful response.
The students wrote a science, response.
The students wrote a careful response, yesterday.
The students, wrote a careful thoughtful response.
guidedPractice.passage: The students revised a paragraph about a class project. They checked each sentence for clear meaning, correct punctuation, and words that matched the formal style of the report.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which sentence uses a comma correctly between coordinate adjectives?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: Careful and thoughtful both describe response.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: The students wrote a careful, thoughtful response.
