standardLabel: Maintain formal style and precise language
title: Grade 7 Formal Style and Precise Language Lesson
lessonExplanation: Academic writing should maintain a formal style and use precise language. Replace vague or casual wording with specific, appropriate language.
workedExample: Revise 'The plan was super good' to 'The plan was effective because it reduced waste and saved time.'

choices: The results suggest that the new schedule increased participation.
The new schedule was awesome and everyone liked it a ton.
The thing worked pretty good.
It was kind of a big deal.

standardLabel: Conventions of Standard English - Formal and Informal Style
title: Grade 7 Formal and Informal Style Lesson
lessonExplanation: Conventions questions ask you to make writing clear and correct. Read the whole paragraph first, then check pronouns, verb tense, punctuation, and style in context.
workedExample: If a sentence says, "A writer should reread the draft because you may notice missing evidence," the pronoun shifts from "a writer" to "you." A better revision keeps the same person: "A writer should reread the draft because the writer may notice missing evidence."

choices: Sentence 2 should be revised to maintain formal style.
Sentence 1 should remove the word students.
Sentence 3 should change class to classes.
Sentence 4 should use informal language.
guidedPractice.passage: (1) When students revise an essay, they should check whether each detail supports the claim. (2) A writer should reread the draft because you may notice missing evidence. (3) The class discussed its ideas before writing the final paragraph. (4) Clear organization helps readers follow the explanation.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Read the paragraph. Which sentence should be revised?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Check whether the sentence keeps the same person and style from beginning to end.
guidedPractice.explanation: Sentence 2 shifts from a general noun to you. Keeping pronouns consistent makes the writing clearer and more formal.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Sentence 2 should be revised to maintain formal style.

standardLabel: Use modifiers clearly and correctly
title: Grade 7 Misplaced and Dangling Modifiers Lesson
lessonExplanation: Modifiers should be placed near the words they describe. Misplaced or dangling modifiers can make a sentence unclear or unintentionally funny.
workedExample: Revise 'Running down the hallway, the backpack fell open' to 'Running down the hallway, Talia noticed her backpack had fallen open.'

choices: Carrying the display board, Malik entered the science fair.
Carrying the display board, the science fair opened.
The science fair, carrying the display board, opened.
Entered the display board Malik carrying.
guidedPractice.passage: The students revised a paragraph about a class project. They checked each sentence for clear meaning, correct punctuation, and words that matched the formal style of the report.

guidedPractice.explanation: The modifier clearly describes Malik.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Carrying the display board, Malik entered the science fair.

standardLabel: Use phrases and clauses for clear writing
title: Grade 7 Phrases and Clauses Lesson
lessonExplanation: Phrases and clauses add detail and variety. Use them to combine ideas while keeping relationships clear.
workedExample: Because the data changed, the team revised its conclusion shows a clear cause-and-effect relationship.

choices: Although the evidence was limited, the team explained its conclusion carefully.
Although the evidence was limited.
The team although explained evidence.
Limited although evidence the team.
guidedPractice.passage: The students revised a paragraph about a class project. They checked each sentence for clear meaning, correct punctuation, and words that matched the formal style of the report.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which sentence uses a dependent clause correctly?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: The dependent clause is connected to a complete sentence.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Although the evidence was limited, the team explained its conclusion carefully.

standardLabel: Conventions of Standard English - Pronoun Agreement and Shifts
title: Grade 7 Pronoun Agreement and Shifts Lesson
lessonExplanation: Conventions questions ask you to make writing clear and correct. Read the whole paragraph first, then check pronouns, verb tense, punctuation, and style in context.
workedExample: If a sentence says, "A writer should reread the draft because you may notice missing evidence," the pronoun shifts from "a writer" to "you." A better revision keeps the same person: "A writer should reread the draft because the writer may notice missing evidence."

choices: Sentence 2 contains an inappropriate pronoun shift.
Sentence 1 should remove the word students.
Sentence 3 should change class to classes.
Sentence 4 should use informal language.
guidedPractice.passage: (1) When students revise an essay, they should check whether each detail supports the claim. (2) A writer should reread the draft because you may notice missing evidence. (3) The class discussed its ideas before writing the final paragraph. (4) Clear organization helps readers follow the explanation.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Read the paragraph. Which sentence should be revised?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Check whether the sentence keeps the same person and style from beginning to end.
guidedPractice.explanation: Sentence 2 shifts from a general noun to you. Keeping pronouns consistent makes the writing clearer and more formal.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Sentence 2 contains an inappropriate pronoun shift.

choices: Sentence 2 contains an inappropriate pronoun shift.

standardLabel: Write arguments with claims, reasons, and counterclaims
title: Grade 7 Argument Counterclaim Response Lesson
lessonExplanation: Grade 7 argument writing should acknowledge an opposing claim and respond with evidence or reasoning.
workedExample: Although some students worry later due dates reduce urgency, a planning checkpoint can keep work on schedule while allowing more time for revision.

choices: Some argue that outdoor lunch is distracting, but a supervised schedule can limit noise while giving students a needed break.
Outdoor lunch is good.
Some people disagree.
The cafeteria has tables.
guidedPractice.passage: A good response stays focused on the prompt. It gives a clear idea, includes details from the text, and explains why those details matter.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which sentence best responds to a counterclaim?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: This sentence acknowledges a concern and responds with reasoning.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Some argue that outdoor lunch is distracting, but a supervised schedule can limit noise while giving students a needed break.

standardLabel: Use evidence and analysis in text-dependent writing
title: Grade 7 TDA Evidence and Explanation Lesson
lessonExplanation: A strong TDA does not retell the passage. It answers the prompt with a clear claim, uses evidence from the text, and explains how the evidence proves the claim.
workedExample: Claim: The character learns to be more responsible. Evidence: The character returns to fix the mistake without being asked. Explanation: This detail matters because the character chooses responsibility even when no one is forcing the choice.

choices: Add a sentence that explains how the evidence proves the claim.
Copy a longer sentence from the passage.
Start a new paragraph with no evidence.
Replace the claim with a summary of the story.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which revision best teaches a Grade 7 writer to explain evidence instead of just listing it?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Use because to connect the evidence back to the claim.
guidedPractice.explanation: A TDA needs evidence and analysis. The explanation shows why the evidence matters.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Add a sentence that explains how the evidence proves the claim.

standardLabel: Conduct short research and gather relevant information
title: Grade 7 Research Questions and Source Notes Lesson
lessonExplanation: Strong research begins with a focused question and notes that capture relevant facts in the student's own words.
workedExample: Instead of asking 'What about recycling?' ask 'How does cafeteria composting affect school waste over one semester?'

choices: How did one middle school's composting program change cafeteria waste during the spring semester?
What is trash?
Do people like food?
Why are schools places?

standardLabel: Analyze central idea development and objective summary
title: Grade 8 Main Idea Lesson
lessonExplanation: Main idea is what the paragraph, section, or whole passage is mostly about. Grade 8 target: Evaluate how central ideas are refined by evidence, structure, and purpose. Text complexity: Complex informational text with layered claims, counterpoints, or author emphasis. Evidence demand: Use precise evidence tied to structure and purpose. Reasoning: Evaluate how the author refines or complicates the central idea.
workedExample: A Grade 8 answer evaluates refinement: the text begins with water conservation, then refines that idea into evidence-based community problem solving.

choices: The central idea develops from saving water to using evidence for broader community problem solving.
The text argues that tools alone solve every problem.
The text mostly lists unrelated school supplies.
The central idea stays simple and unchanged.
guidedPractice.passage: A student research committee studied whether a redesigned schoolyard could serve both environmental and instructional goals. The report first argues that water conservation depends on measurable systems, such as rain collection and drip irrigation. It then refines that claim by showing that systems alone are not enough: students must interpret data, revise routines, and consider maintenance over time. The central idea develops from a simple conservation goal into a broader argument about evidence-based community problem solving.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Evaluate how central ideas are refined by evidence, structure, and purpose.
guidedPractice.coachHint: Use precise evidence tied to structure and purpose.
guidedPractice.explanation: The best answer is "The central idea develops from saving water to using evidence for broader community problem solving." because it meets this Grade 8 expectation: Explain how evidence, structure, and purpose work together to refine central ideas.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: The central idea develops from saving water to using evidence for broader community problem solving.

It then refines that claim by showing that systems alone are not enough: students must interpret data, revise routines, and consider maintenance over time. The central idea develops from a simple conservation goal into a broader argument about evidence-based community problem solving.
independentPractice.question: Independent 4: Evaluate how central ideas are refined by evidence, structure, and purpose.
independentPractice.coachHint: Use precise evidence tied to structure and purpose.
independentPractice.explanation: The best answer is "The central idea develops from saving water to using evidence for broader community problem solving." because it meets this Grade 8 expectation: Explain how evidence, structure, and purpose work together to refine central ideas. This item adds another chance to apply the same skill with the Grade 8 evidence expectation.
independentPractice.correctAnswer: The central idea develops from saving water to using evidence for broader community problem solving.

standardLabel: Analyze multiple central ideas and their development
title: Grade 8 Multiple Central Ideas Lesson
lessonExplanation: Grade 8 readers often track more than one central idea. Analyze how the ideas interact, which evidence develops each idea, and how the author brings them together.
workedExample: An article about urban gardens may develop one idea about food access and another about community leadership, then show how both ideas support the author's conclusion.

choices: The author develops one idea about limited resources and another about student leadership, then connects them through the school garden project.
The text has two long paragraphs.
The title tells readers the topic.
The author mentions students twice.
guidedPractice.passage: A school compost bin can turn fruit peels and leaves into rich soil. Students add plant scraps, mix the pile, and keep it slightly damp. Over time, tiny living things break down the scraps. The soil can help a garden grow.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which answer best analyzes two central ideas in a text?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: This answer explains two ideas and how they connect.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: The author develops one idea about limited resources and another about student leadership, then connects them through the school garden project.

standardLabel: Draw inferences supported by strong textual evidence
title: Grade 8 Inference Lesson
lessonExplanation: Inference means using text clues plus what makes sense to figure out an idea the author does not state directly. Grade 8 target: Evaluate complex inferences across structure, tone, and evidence. Text complexity: Complex text with competing clues or nuanced viewpoint. Evidence demand: Weigh the strongest evidence. Reasoning: Distinguish supported inference from plausible but weak claims.
workedExample: If the committee connects systems, data, revision, and maintenance, you can infer they see the redesign as both practical and educational.

choices: The committee sees conservation as both a technical and instructional challenge.
The committee believes data is unnecessary.
The report dismisses maintenance.
The redesign has no educational purpose.
guidedPractice.passage: A student research committee studied whether a redesigned schoolyard could serve both environmental and instructional goals. The report first argues that water conservation depends on measurable systems, such as rain collection and drip irrigation. It then refines that claim by showing that systems alone are not enough: students must interpret data, revise routines, and consider maintenance over time. The central idea develops from a simple conservation goal into a broader argument about evidence-based community problem solving.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Evaluate complex inferences across structure, tone, and evidence.
guidedPractice.coachHint: Weigh the strongest evidence.
guidedPractice.explanation: The best answer is "The committee sees conservation as both a technical and instructional challenge." because it meets this Grade 8 expectation: Defend a complex inference using the most relevant evidence.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: The committee sees conservation as both a technical and instructional challenge.

masteryCheck.explanation: The best answer is "The committee sees conservation as both a technical and instructional challenge." because it meets this Grade 8 expectation: Defend a complex inference using the most relevant evidence. masteryCheck.correctAnswer: The committee sees conservation as both a technical and instructional challenge.

standardLabel: Cite the strongest textual evidence to support analysis
title: Grade 8 Text Evidence Lesson
lessonExplanation: Text evidence is the detail, sentence, event, or quotation from the passage that proves an answer. Grade 8 target: Evaluate which evidence most strongly supports analysis. Text complexity: Complex text with competing or nuanced evidence. Evidence demand: Compare evidence quality and relevance. Reasoning: Explain why selected evidence is strongest.
workedExample: For the claim that the central idea is refined, cite the shift from measurable systems to interpreting data, revising routines, and considering maintenance.

choices: The report refines the claim by showing students must interpret data, revise routines, and consider maintenance over time.
The committee studied a schoolyard.
The report mentions systems.
The text includes the word goals.
guidedPractice.passage: A student research committee studied whether a redesigned schoolyard could serve both environmental and instructional goals. The report first argues that water conservation depends on measurable systems, such as rain collection and drip irrigation. It then refines that claim by showing that systems alone are not enough: students must interpret data, revise routines, and consider maintenance over time. The central idea develops from a simple conservation goal into a broader argument about evidence-based community problem solving.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Evaluate which evidence most strongly supports analysis.
guidedPractice.coachHint: Compare evidence quality and relevance.
guidedPractice.explanation: The best answer is "The report refines the claim by showing students must interpret data, revise routines, and consider maintenance over time." because it meets this Grade 8 expectation: Defend analysis with precise and relevant evidence.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: The report refines the claim by showing students must interpret data, revise routines, and consider maintenance over time.

standardLabel: Analyze author's point of view and bias
title: Grade 8 Author Perspective and Bias Lesson
lessonExplanation: Perspective and bias influence which details an author includes, emphasizes, or leaves out. Analyze language, evidence choices, and missing viewpoints.
workedExample: An author who only quotes supporters of a proposal may be presenting a limited perspective.

choices: The author quotes three supporters of the policy but no critics and calls the opposing view foolish.
The article includes a date.
The text has a chart.
The passage explains a vocabulary word.
guidedPractice.passage: A school compost bin can turn fruit peels and leaves into rich soil. Students add plant scraps, mix the pile, and keep it slightly damp. Over time, tiny living things break down the scraps. The soil can help a garden grow.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which detail most clearly suggests author bias?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: One-sided sources and loaded language can reveal bias.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: The author quotes three supporters of the policy but no critics and calls the opposing view foolish.

standardLabel: Analyze and compare text structures
title: Grade 8 Compare Text Structures Lesson
lessonExplanation: Compare how different structures shape meaning. Ask how chronology, comparison, cause and effect, or problem and solution changes what readers notice.
workedExample: A chronological article emphasizes how an issue changed over time, while a cause-and-effect article emphasizes why the issue happened.

choices: The first text uses chronology to show change over time, while the second uses cause and effect to explain why the change occurred.
Both texts use commas.
One text has more words.
Both texts are informational.
guidedPractice.passage: A school compost bin can turn fruit peels and leaves into rich soil. Students add plant scraps, mix the pile, and keep it slightly damp. Over time, tiny living things break down the scraps. The soil can help a garden grow.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which comparison best analyzes two text structures?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: This compares how structure affects the presentation of ideas.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: The first text uses chronology to show change over time, while the second uses cause and effect to explain why the change occurred.

standardLabel: Analyze visual and quantitative information
title: Grade 8 Visual and Quantitative Evidence Lesson
lessonExplanation: Visual and quantitative information can strengthen, complicate, or challenge a written claim. Connect data to the author's reasoning.
workedExample: If a graph shows participation decreased after a schedule change, it may challenge an author's claim that the change helped students.

choices: It can show data that partly supports the claim but also reveals an exception or limitation.
It always repeats the author's exact words.
It replaces the need to read the passage.
It proves every claim is false.
guidedPractice.passage: A school compost bin can turn fruit peels and leaves into rich soil. Students add plant scraps, mix the pile, and keep it slightly damp. Over time, tiny living things break down the scraps. The soil can help a garden grow.

standardLabel: Evaluate reasoning, appeals, and fallacies
title: Grade 8 Logical Fallacies and Appeals Lesson
lessonExplanation: Writers may use emotional appeals, weak reasoning, or fallacies. Identify whether the evidence actually proves the claim or merely tries to influence the reader.
workedExample: The statement 'Everyone supports this plan, so it must be best' uses popularity as evidence instead of showing why the plan works.

choices: This policy must be effective because many people shared the post online.
The policy reduced waste by 18 percent during a six-week pilot.
The survey included students from all three grade levels.
The report compares results before and after the change.
guidedPractice.passage: A school compost bin can turn fruit peels and leaves into rich soil. Students add plant scraps, mix the pile, and keep it slightly damp. Over time, tiny living things break down the scraps. The soil can help a garden grow.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which statement relies on weak reasoning?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: Popularity does not prove effectiveness.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: This policy must be effective because many people shared the post online.

standardLabel: Trace and evaluate argument and evidence
title: Grade 8 Trace and Evaluate Arguments Lesson
lessonExplanation: Grade 8 argument analysis asks students to trace a claim, evaluate whether reasons are logical, and decide whether evidence is relevant and sufficient.
workedExample: A claim about reducing food waste is stronger when it includes data from the cafeteria, explains the cause of waste, and addresses practical limits.

choices: The claim is mostly supported because the author uses relevant survey data, but the argument would be stronger with evidence from more than one school.
The claim is true because it sounds confident.
The evidence is strong because it appears near the end.
The argument is weak because the title is short.

guidedPractice.explanation: This answer evaluates relevance and sufficiency of evidence.

independentPractice.passage: A school compost bin can turn fruit peels and leaves into rich soil. Students add plant scraps, mix the pile, and keep it slightly damp. Over time, tiny living things break down the scraps. The soil can help a garden grow.

independentPractice.correctAnswer: The claim is mostly supported because the author uses relevant survey data, but the argument would be stronger with evidence from more than one school.

standardLabel: Analyze conflicting information across texts
title: Grade 8 Conflicting Information Across Texts Lesson
lessonExplanation: Texts on the same topic may present conflicting information. Compare the authors' evidence, dates, sources, and purpose before deciding how to interpret the disagreement.
workedExample: One article may claim a plan saved money, while another says costs increased. A reader should compare what each author measured and when.

choices: Compare the evidence, source, date, and purpose of each text.
Believe the shorter text automatically.
Ignore both texts.
Choose the text with the more interesting title.
guidedPractice.passage: A school compost bin can turn fruit peels and leaves into rich soil. Students add plant scraps, mix the pile, and keep it slightly damp. Over time, tiny living things break down the scraps. The soil can help a garden grow.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: What should a reader do when two texts give conflicting information?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: Conflicting information should be evaluated through evidence and source context.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Compare the evidence, source, date, and purpose of each text.

standardLabel: Analyze multiple themes and their development
title: Grade 8 Multiple Themes in Literature Lesson
lessonExplanation: Literary texts can develop more than one theme. Analyze how conflicts, symbols, character choices, and endings develop related messages.
workedExample: A story may develop themes about ambition and loyalty when a character must choose between personal success and helping a friend.

choices: The story develops a theme about ambition through the contest and a theme about loyalty through the character's final choice.
The story has two characters.
The setting changes once.
The author uses quotation marks.
guidedPractice.passage: Nora wanted to build the tallest block tower in the room. When it fell twice, she took a breath, sorted the blocks by size, and tried again. Her friend Eli held the base steady. This time, the tower stood until cleanup.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which answer best analyzes multiple themes?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: This answer identifies two themes and the evidence that develops each.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: The story develops a theme about ambition through the contest and a theme about loyalty through the character's final choice.

standardLabel: Analyze theme development and relationships
title: Grade 8 Theme Lesson
lessonExplanation: Theme is the message or lesson a story suggests about life. A theme is usually a complete idea, not one word. Grade 8 target: Evaluate theme development and relationship to structure. Text complexity: Complex literary text with symbolism, shifts, or layered conflict. Evidence demand: Use evidence from structure, dialogue, and events. Reasoning: Explain nuanced or multiple themes.
workedExample: A stronger upper-grade theme explains a larger idea: responsible problem solving requires evidence, revision, and cooperation.

choices: Responsible problem solving grows from evidence, revision, and cooperation.
Fast answers are always best.
Community problems cannot be solved.
Data makes decisions weaker.
guidedPractice.passage: A student research committee studied whether a redesigned schoolyard could serve both environmental and instructional goals. The report first argues that water conservation depends on measurable systems, such as rain collection and drip irrigation. It then refines that claim by showing that systems alone are not enough: students must interpret data, revise routines, and consider maintenance over time. The central idea develops from a simple conservation goal into a broader argument about evidence-based community problem solving.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Evaluate theme development and relationship to structure.
guidedPractice.coachHint: Use evidence from structure, dialogue, and events.
guidedPractice.explanation: The best answer is "Responsible problem solving grows from evidence, revision, and cooperation." because it meets this Grade 8 expectation: Evaluate how the author develops theme through craft choices.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Responsible problem solving grows from evidence, revision, and cooperation.

choices: Responsible problem solving grows from evidence, revision, and cooperation.
Fast answers are always best.
Community problems cannot be solved.
Data makes decisions weaker.
guidedPractice.passage: A student research committee studied whether a redesigned schoolyard could serve both environmental and instructional goals. The report first argues that water conservation depends on measurable systems, such as rain collection and drip irrigation. It then refines that claim by showing that systems alone are not enough: students must interpret data, revise routines, and consider maintenance over time. The central idea develops from a simple conservation goal into a broader argument about evidence-based community problem solving.
guidedPractice.question: Guided Evidence: Evaluate theme development and relationship to structure.
guidedPractice.coachHint: Use evidence from structure, dialogue, and events.
guidedPractice.explanation: The best answer is "Responsible problem solving grows from evidence, revision, and cooperation." because it meets this Grade 8 expectation: Evaluate how the author develops theme through craft choices.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Responsible problem solving grows from evidence, revision, and cooperation.

choices: Responsible problem solving grows from evidence, revision, and cooperation.
Fast answers are always best.
Community problems cannot be solved.
Data makes decisions weaker.
guidedPractice.passage: A student research committee studied whether a redesigned schoolyard could serve both environmental and instructional goals. The report first argues that water conservation depends on measurable systems, such as rain collection and drip irrigation. It then refines that claim by showing that systems alone are not enough: students must interpret data, revise routines, and consider maintenance over time. The central idea develops from a simple conservation goal into a broader argument about evidence-based community problem solving.

standardLabel: Analyze dialogue and incidents that reveal character
title: Grade 8 Dialogue and Characterization Lesson
lessonExplanation: Dialogue can reveal motive, conflict, relationships, and character change. Analyze what a character says, what is implied, and how others respond.
workedExample: A character who says 'I do not need anyone' while accepting help may reveal pride and internal conflict.

choices: 'I can fix this myself,' Mara snapped, even as she studied the directions her friend had written.
Mara walked into the room.
The directions were on paper.
The story begins after school.
guidedPractice.passage: Nora wanted to build the tallest block tower in the room. When it fell twice, she took a breath, sorted the blocks by size, and tried again. Her friend Eli held the base steady. This time, the tower stood until cleanup.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which detail best shows dialogue revealing character?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: The dialogue and action reveal pride and conflict.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: 'I can fix this myself,' Mara snapped, even as she studied the directions her friend had written.

standardLabel: Analyze literary elements and plot effects
title: Grade 8 Dramatic Irony and Suspense Lesson
lessonExplanation: Dramatic irony occurs when readers know something a character does not. Authors use it to create suspense, humor, or tension.
workedExample: If readers know the missing letter is in the character's backpack, every failed search can become more suspenseful.

choices: Readers know important information that a character does not, so they anticipate what may happen next.
Readers and characters know exactly the same information.
The author avoids conflict.
The setting becomes less important.
guidedPractice.passage: Nora wanted to build the tallest block tower in the room. When it fell twice, she took a breath, sorted the blocks by size, and tried again. Her friend Eli held the base steady. This time, the tower stood until cleanup.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: How does dramatic irony create suspense?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: The gap between reader knowledge and character knowledge builds tension.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Readers know important information that a character does not, so they anticipate what may happen next.

standardLabel: Analyze how point of view creates effects
title: Grade 8 Point of View Lesson
lessonExplanation: Point of view is who is telling the story or how an author sees a topic. Grade 8 target: Evaluate conflicting viewpoints or narrator reliability. Text complexity: Complex text with multiple perspectives or unreliable narration. Evidence demand: Compare evidence across viewpoints. Reasoning: Evaluate how viewpoint affects meaning and trust.
workedExample: The author emphasizes evidence, revision, and realistic maintenance, revealing a point of view that values practical problem solving.

choices: The author presents evidence-based problem solving as valuable and realistic.
The author treats the project as a joke.
The author avoids explaining the recommendation.
The author believes routines do not matter.
guidedPractice.passage: A student research committee studied whether a redesigned schoolyard could serve both environmental and instructional goals. The report first argues that water conservation depends on measurable systems, such as rain collection and drip irrigation. It then refines that claim by showing that systems alone are not enough: students must interpret data, revise routines, and consider maintenance over time. The central idea develops from a simple conservation goal into a broader argument about evidence-based community problem solving.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Evaluate conflicting viewpoints or narrator reliability.
guidedPractice.coachHint: Compare evidence across viewpoints.
guidedPractice.explanation: The best answer is "The author presents evidence-based problem solving as valuable and realistic." because it meets this Grade 8 expectation: Explain how conflicting perspectives shape interpretation.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: The author presents evidence-based problem solving as valuable and realistic.

standardLabel: Analyze figurative, connotative, and technical meanings
title: Grade 8 Connotation and Figurative Language Lesson
lessonExplanation: Connotation and figurative language shape meaning. Look for words or phrases that suggest feelings, images, or nonliteral ideas, then explain their effect. Grade 8 target: Evaluate symbolism, tone, and author purpose in word choice. Text complexity: Complex text with symbolic or layered language. Evidence demand: Use precise word-choice evidence. Reasoning: Evaluate why the author chose specific language.
workedExample: If an author calls a hallway "a river of students," the phrase does not mean real water. It suggests many students moving quickly together. Grade 8 expectation: Explain how language choices shape theme, tone, or purpose.

choices: It uses a comparison to shape tone and help readers picture the movement.
It is a literal statement about water.
It weakens the image.
It proves the author dislikes students.

The report first argues that water conservation depends on measurable systems, such as rain collection and drip irrigation. The central idea develops from a simple conservation goal into a broader argument about evidence-based community problem solving.

standardLabel: Analyze the effect of figurative language
title: Grade 8 Figurative Language Effect Lesson
lessonExplanation: Grade 8 analysis should explain the effect of figurative language, not just identify it. Ask what image, tone, or idea the language creates.
workedExample: If a writer says a rumor spread like smoke, the simile suggests something hard to contain and difficult to see clearly.

choices: It suggests the memory is hidden, difficult to enter, or emotionally protected.
It proves the character owns a key.
It means the setting is a house.
It shows the sentence is nonfiction.
guidedPractice.passage: Nora wanted to build the tallest block tower in the room. When it fell twice, she took a breath, sorted the blocks by size, and tried again. Her friend Eli held the base steady. This time, the tower stood until cleanup.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: What is the effect of describing a memory as a locked room?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: The figurative image creates a deeper idea about memory.

standardLabel: Compare texts across genres on themes and patterns
title: Grade 8 Compare Genres on Similar Themes Lesson
lessonExplanation: Different genres can develop similar themes in different ways. Compare how a poem, story, drama, or myth uses structure, speaker, character, and conflict.
workedExample: A poem may develop perseverance through repeated images, while a story may develop it through a character's choices.

choices: The poem develops resilience through repeated images, while the story develops resilience through a character's decision during conflict.
Both texts are written in English.
One text is shorter.
Both texts have titles.
guidedPractice.passage: Nora wanted to build the tallest block tower in the room. When it fell twice, she took a breath, sorted the blocks by size, and tried again. Her friend Eli held the base steady. This time, the tower stood until cleanup.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which comparison best analyzes two genres?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: This comparison explains how genre affects theme development.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: The poem develops resilience through repeated images, while the story develops resilience through a character's decision during conflict.

standardLabel: Write informative texts that synthesize ideas
title: Grade 8 Informative Synthesis Writing Lesson
lessonExplanation: Synthesis writing combines ideas from multiple sources into a clear explanation. Organize by idea, not by simply listing one source after another.
workedExample: A synthesis about water conservation might group evidence by household use, school use, and community planning while citing multiple sources in each section.

choices: Group source information by shared ideas, explain patterns, and use evidence from more than one source in each section.
Summarize source one, then source two, with no connection.
Copy the longest sentence from each source.
Use only the source with the easiest vocabulary.
guidedPractice.passage: A good response stays focused on the prompt. It gives a clear idea, includes details from the text, and explains why those details matter.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which plan best supports informative synthesis?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: Synthesis combines sources around ideas and relationships.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Group source information by shared ideas, explain patterns, and use evidence from more than one source in each section.

standardLabel: Apply punctuation for clarity and effect
title: Grade 8 Ellipses, Dashes, and Punctuation Effects Lesson
lessonExplanation: Punctuation can clarify relationships and create emphasis. Use dashes, ellipses, and commas intentionally rather than randomly.
workedExample: The final design--lighter, cheaper, and stronger--surprised the judges uses dashes to emphasize extra information.

choices: The solution--simple but unexpected--changed the team's results.
The solution simple--but unexpected changed--the team's results.
The--solution simple but unexpected changed the team's results.
The solution simple but unexpected--changed--the team's results.
guidedPractice.passage: The students revised a paragraph about a class project. They checked each sentence for clear meaning, correct punctuation, and words that matched the formal style of the report.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which sentence uses dashes effectively?

standardLabel: Maintain formal academic style and precise language
title: Grade 8 Formal Academic Style Lesson
lessonExplanation: Formal academic style uses precise nouns, strong verbs, and objective language. Avoid vague, casual, or exaggerated wording.
workedExample: Revise 'The article totally proves the plan is great' to 'The article provides evidence that the plan reduced waste during the trial period.'

choices: The evidence indicates that the pilot program reduced waste during lunch periods.
The program was super amazing and everyone loved it.
The thing worked a bunch.
It was basically awesome.

guidedPractice.explanation: This sentence is precise, formal, and evidence-focused.

standardLabel: Conventions of Standard English - Formal and Informal Style
title: Grade 8 Formal and Informal Style Lesson
lessonExplanation: Conventions questions ask you to make writing clear and correct. Read the whole paragraph first, then check pronouns, verb tense, punctuation, and style in context.
workedExample: If a sentence says, "A writer should reread the draft because you may notice missing evidence," the pronoun shifts from "a writer" to "you." A better revision keeps the same person: "A writer should reread the draft because the writer may notice missing evidence."

choices: Sentence 2 should be revised to maintain formal style.
Sentence 1 should remove the word students.
Sentence 3 should change class to classes.
Sentence 4 should use informal language.
guidedPractice.passage: (1) When students revise an essay, they should check whether each detail supports the claim. (2) A writer should reread the draft because you may notice missing evidence. (3) The class discussed its ideas before writing the final paragraph. (4) Clear organization helps readers follow the explanation.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Read the paragraph. Which sentence should be revised?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Check whether the sentence keeps the same person and style from beginning to end.
guidedPractice.explanation: Sentence 2 shifts from a general noun to you. Keeping pronouns consistent makes the writing clearer and more formal.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Sentence 2 should be revised to maintain formal style.

standardLabel: Use parallel structure for clarity and style
title: Grade 8 Parallel Structure Lesson
lessonExplanation: Parallel structure uses matching grammatical patterns to make lists and comparisons clear.
workedExample: The plan requires collecting data, interviewing students, and revising the schedule uses parallel -ing phrases.

choices: The project requires planning carefully, testing often, and revising honestly.
The project requires planning carefully, frequent tests, and to revise honestly.
The project requires careful planning, testing often, and honest.
Planning carefully, tests, and revision are required.
guidedPractice.passage: The students revised a paragraph about a class project. They checked each sentence for clear meaning, correct punctuation, and words that matched the formal style of the report.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which sentence uses parallel structure?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: The three items use the same grammatical pattern.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: The project requires planning carefully, testing often, and revising honestly.

standardLabel: Conventions of Standard English - Pronoun Agreement and Shifts
title: Grade 8 Pronoun Agreement and Shifts Lesson
lessonExplanation: Conventions questions ask you to make writing clear and correct. Read the whole paragraph first, then check pronouns, verb tense, punctuation, and style in context.
workedExample: If a sentence says, "A writer should reread the draft because you may notice missing evidence," the pronoun shifts from "a writer" to "you." A better revision keeps the same person: "A writer should reread the draft because the writer may notice missing evidence."

choices: Sentence 2 contains an inappropriate pronoun shift.
Sentence 1 should remove the word students.
Sentence 3 should change class to classes.
Sentence 4 should use informal language.
guidedPractice.passage: (1) When students revise an essay, they should check whether each detail supports the claim. (2) A writer should reread the draft because you may notice missing evidence. (3) The class discussed its ideas before writing the final paragraph. (4) Clear organization helps readers follow the explanation.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Read the paragraph. Which sentence should be revised?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Check whether the sentence keeps the same person and style from beginning to end.
guidedPractice.explanation: Sentence 2 shifts from a general noun to you. Keeping pronouns consistent makes the writing clearer and more formal.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Sentence 2 contains an inappropriate pronoun shift.

standardLabel: Use verbs in active/passive voice and mood
title: Grade 8 Verb Moods and Voice Lesson
lessonExplanation: Verb voice and mood affect clarity and meaning. Active voice often makes the actor clear; passive voice may emphasize the action or result. Verb mood shows purpose or attitude.
workedExample: Active: The committee approved the plan. Passive: The plan was approved by the committee.

choices: The team revised the proposal after reviewing the data.
The proposal was revised by the team.
The data was reviewed by the committee.
The schedule was changed yesterday.
guidedPractice.passage: The students revised a paragraph about a class project. They checked each sentence for clear meaning, correct punctuation, and words that matched the formal style of the report.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which sentence uses active voice?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: The subject, team, performs the action.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: The team revised the proposal after reviewing the data.

standardLabel: Write arguments with claims, counterclaims, and evidence
title: Grade 8 Argument with Counterclaims and Evidence Lesson
lessonExplanation: Grade 8 argument writing should develop a clear claim, acknowledge counterclaims fairly, and use strong evidence with explanation.
workedExample: A strong paragraph might admit that a plan costs money, then explain how long-term savings and student benefits outweigh the initial cost.

choices: Although the program requires training time, the evidence shows that trained peer tutors improved completion rates over the semester.
Some people disagree.
The program is good because it is good.
Training is a word in the article.
guidedPractice.passage: A good response stays focused on the prompt. It gives a clear idea, includes details from the text, and explains why those details matter.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which sentence best integrates a counterclaim into an argument?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: This sentence fairly acknowledges a concern and responds with evidence.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Although the program requires training time, the evidence shows that trained peer tutors improved completion rates over the semester.

choices: Although the program requires training time, the evidence shows that trained peer tutors improved completion rates over the semester.

standardLabel: Use strong evidence and analysis in text-dependent writing
title: Grade 8 TDA Evidence and Explanation Lesson
lessonExplanation: A strong TDA does not retell the passage. It answers the prompt with a clear claim, uses evidence from the text, and explains how the evidence proves the claim.
workedExample: Claim: The character learns to be more responsible. Evidence: The character returns to fix the mistake without being asked. Explanation: This detail matters because the character chooses responsibility even when no one is forcing the choice.

choices: Add a sentence that explains how the evidence proves the claim.
Copy a longer sentence from the passage.
Start a new paragraph with no evidence.
Replace the claim with a summary of the story.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which revision best teaches a Grade 8 writer to explain evidence instead of just listing it?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Use because to connect the evidence back to the claim.
guidedPractice.explanation: A TDA needs evidence and analysis. The explanation shows why the evidence matters.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: Add a sentence that explains how the evidence proves the claim.

standardLabel: Assess source credibility and integrate research
title: Grade 8 Research Source Credibility Lesson
lessonExplanation: Credible sources are relevant, accurate, current, and supported by evidence. Evaluate who created the source, why it was created, and what evidence it uses.
workedExample: A recent report from a local water authority is usually stronger evidence for water-use data than an anonymous opinion post.

choices: A recent report from the local weather service with monthly rainfall totals
An anonymous comment saying it feels rainy
A story about a rainy vacation
A poster with no date or source
guidedPractice.passage: A good response stays focused on the prompt. It gives a clear idea, includes details from the text, and explains why those details matter.
guidedPractice.question: Guided: Which source would likely be most credible for data about local rainfall?
guidedPractice.coachHint: Read the whole sentence or short passage before choosing.
guidedPractice.explanation: The source is current, relevant, and evidence-based.
guidedPractice.correctAnswer: A recent report from the local weather service with monthly rainfall totals
